Q2 2011/12 Tata Consultancy Services Ltd Earnings Conference Call - Final https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/q2-2011-12-tata-consultancy-services-ltd-earnings/docview/901198439/se-2?accountid=27562 Abstract: None available. Full text: Presentation OPERATOR: Ladies and gentlemen, good day and welcome to the TCS earnings conference call. As a reminder, for the duration of this conference all participant lines will be in the listen-only mode and there will be an opportunity for you to ask questions at the end of today's presentation. (Operator Instructions). I would now like to hand the conference over to Mr. Kedar Shirali of TCS. Thank you and over to you, sir. KEDAR SHIRALI, HEAD GLOBAL INVESTOR RELATIONS, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Thank you, Melissa. Good evening and welcome, everyone. Thank you for joining us today to discuss TCS's financial results for the second quarter of fiscal year 2012 that ended September 30, 2011. This call is being broadcast through our website, and an archive including the transcript will be available on the site for the duration of this quarter. The financial statements, results presentation and press releases are also available on our website, www.tcs.com. Our leadership team is present on this call to discuss our results. We have with us today Mr. N. Chandrasekaran, Chief Executive Officer and Managing Director. N. CHANDRASEKARAN, CEO & MD, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Good morning to you all. KEDAR SHIRALI: Mr. S. Mahalingam, Chief Financial Officer and Executive Director. S. MAHALINGAM, CHIEF FINANCIAL OFFICER AND EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Good evening, good morning. KEDAR SHIRALI: Mr. Phiroz A. Vandrevala, Director. PHIROZ VANDREVALA, DIRECTOR, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Hi, everyone. KEDAR SHIRALI: And Mr. Ajoy Mukherjee, Head of Global Human Resources. AJOY MUKHERJEE, HEAD GLOBAL HUMAN RESOURCES, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Hello, everyone. KEDAR SHIRALI: Chandra and Maha will give us a brief overview of the Company's performance, followed by the Q&A session. As you are aware, we do not provide specific revenue guidance. Anything said on this call which reflects our outlook for the future or which can be construed as a forward-looking statement must be reviewed in conjunction with the risks that the Company faces. We have outlined these risks in

the second slide of the results presentation available on our website. With that, I'd like to turn the call over to Chandra. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Thanks, Kedar. I'm very happy to talk to all of you to report our Q2 results this quarter. Once again we've had a quarter of exceptional performance across the board. This is the sixth straight of quarter where our incremental revenues are in excess of $100m. To start with, we had a very good volume growth, 6.2% on a sequential basis. Our international revenues grew by 9% on a sequential basis. And the overall revenues grew by 7.7% on a sequential basis. Barring India, all our markets delivered very good sequential growth. Importantly, North America grew by 8.8%. UK grew by 10.3%. And Continental Europe grew by 9.3%. And Asia Pacific grew by 9.8%. And Middle East and Africa grew by 9.3%. So, all of our key markets delivered very good results. India had a decline and our Latin America was pretty muted. From an industry point of view, except to telecom, all our industries delivered excellent growth. Particularly, financial services grew 8.6% sequentially, which was very good. Compared to telecom which is a negative 1.7%, all the other verticals, retail grew by 12.2%, manufacturing 10.3%, travel 10.3%, energy 20% and hi-tech and life sciences also grew upwards of 9%. So it has been a well-rounded growth, with volumes coming across sectors and only, from a market point of view, India, and from a sector point of view, telecom, had a negative growth. Then, from a deal-signing point of view, it has been a very, very good quarter. We signed at least 10 deals which are in the range of $100m or more. And these deals came from major markets, North America contributing five and Europe and UK contributing four and one deal coming from Latin America. And with respect to the sectors, two each came from banking and financial services, insurance and telecom, and one each came from the remaining sectors, hi tech, retail, energy and utilities and pharma. And, importantly, the two deals signed

in the telecom are strategic customers, new customers who have a long-term potential. So I'm quite happy with the signings that we had in the telecom sector. Our customer metrics looks very good. We had an excellent migration of customers across all revenue bands. We added to new customers to the $100m category. And $50m category improved by three more customers. And $20m category additional three more customers. And $10m category by seven customers more. And also a significant addition in the $1m and the $5m categories. And in terms of our pipeline, the pipeline looks very healthy. And I've been traveling significantly and met with a large number of customers over the last two months. And across sectors and across markets so far we have not heard any negative news from customers. Our pipeline is very robust. The deal signings are on track. We have not seen any delays. And though the macro environment continues to be concerning, nervous, on the ground all we are hearing from customers are indicating to us that the outlook is quite positive at this point in time. In terms of the investments, there is a significant amount of effort going on across customers to drive efficiency through transformation of their application infrastructure and technology infrastructure as well as enterprise solutions infrastructure. From a discretionary-spend point of view, there is a significant focus in preparing their corporations for the digital consumer, for real-time analytics in decision making using big data technologies, and in terms of innovation for new products in the marketplace, both in terms of time to market and the price of the product, so that they can attract new customer segments. Also cloud is an area where customers are very keen in order to expand their businesses to emerging market, as they do not want to invest and build their own IT systems or transport systems from the major markets. These are all some of the areas where there are significant amount of spending as well as discussions happening. And also from the financial-services sector the outlook is positive. And also there

is a lot of effort going on in compliance-related initiatives as well. Those will be my opening comments and I'll pass it on to Maha. S. MAHALINGAM: Thanks, Chandra. As mentioned already, our revenue of INR116.335b translates into a sequential growth of 7.7% and year-on-year growth of 25.3%. Cross-currency movement clouded the sequential revenue growth in dollar terms. Our revenue of $2.525b translates into a Q-on-Q growth of 4.7% and year-on-year growth of 26%. However in constant currency, revenue growth was similar to last quarter. Constant currency revenue was $2.539b. That's a sequential growth of 5.2%. The breakup of our quarterly revenue growth in Indian rupee terms is as follows. Volume growth, plus 6.25% (sic - see presentation). Exchange, plus 2.49%. Realization, negative 95 basis points. Effort mix, negative 5 basis points. Total 7.7%. Operational leverage from the strong top-line growth including the currency benefit helped us absorb the incremental cost of promotion and all the hiring in this quarter, and expand our EBIT margin by over 0.9% to 27.1%. The impact of different levers on the operating margin follows. Currency, plus 166 basis points. Offshore shift, plus 4 basis points. Rate productivity impact, 73 basis points minus. SG&A efficiencies, provisioning for bad and doubtful debts, 10 basis points plus. Others, minus 13 basis points. Totaling to an increase of 94 basis points. We had unprecedented currency volatility in this quarter. Against last quarter's average rate of INR44.70 against the dollar, the rupee appreciated in July to under INR44, and then swung the (technical difficulty) by approximately 14% to nearly INR50 in September. The resultant ForEx loss, as well as reduced income on a smaller cash base ex-dividend, impacted our other income this quarter. Effective tax rate for the quarter was 24.3%. And net margin stood at 21%. Days outstanding for this quarter was 82 days. Invested funds stood at INR94.5b. In summary, we have seen good growth in those very segments as articulated by Chandra, which we had considered most at risk in this highly uncertain environment. Nevertheless, we continuously -- continue to closely monitor them for early signs

of stress. The extreme volatility in currency is a different matter that continues to remain a matter of concern. In the meantime we continue to expand capacity to fulfill the demand we see in the marketplace. With that we can now open it to questions. Questions and Answers OPERATOR: Thank you. Ladies and gentlemen, we will now begin with the question and answer session. (Operator Instructions). We have the first question from the line of Ankur Rudra from Ambit Capital. Please go ahead. ANKUR RUDRA, ANALYST, AMBIT CAPITAL: Good evening. Thanks for taking my questions. Just one question on the remarkably slow momentum in ADM. Is this a reflection of possibly and actively choosing to look for more transformational work, or reflection of the nature of deals in the pipeline? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Ankur, that's a good question, but actually, if you look at it, it's better to take a view over a few quarters rather than look at only one quarter. These variations will occur. But this quarter, if you really see, we have done extremely well on infrastructure, assurance services and enterprise solutions. We are not consciously looking away from ADM deals. ADM is a very important area. We still feel there is significant opportunity. It's just that this quarter I think we've had three or four other sectors, infrastructure, asset leverage solutions, global consulting as well as BPO, everybody kicking in. So it looks a little lower, but I wouldn't read too much into that. ANKUR RUDRA: Fair enough. Just on the hiring side maybe, clearly it's very impressive levels. And maybe you could comment on the training capacities, given incrementally larger proportion of trainees will be used -- will use those facilities. And where do we stand in terms of future? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: First of all, Ankur, we are operating at a very high utilization, 83.1%. We will continue to be operating at high levels. We will increase it a little bit more so that we will give ourselves room for building capacity. We are continuing to hire. We will hire more. Now from a training capacity

point of view, we have the infrastructure in place to be able to handle the kind of volume of associates we are taking. We have designated five locations in India as the key locations for doing the ILP trainings and we have created associated infrastructure both in terms of training classrooms and also the employee facilities, etc. And, as you know, we are building a new campus in Trivandrum, but that will take a couple of years or more. And also we are investing significantly in terms of digital training and that's something that we are working on. It will take some time, but I think we will change the paradigm in due course, not in short term but maybe I'm talking about a couple of years. ANKUR RUDRA: Just one last question on cash conversion, if I can. Clearly cash conversion has improved quite a bit sequentially. However, if I notice, the DSO and the unbilled DSO has continued to creep up a bit. Is there any internal benchmark where you keep track of that -- at which level you're comfortable with and beyond which you won't be. And also maybe you can add color to what led to this rise. S. MAHALINGAM: See, as far as the DSO is concerned, we report it in dollar terms now, and that has actually come down from 84 days to 82 days. So I think, from a cash position, there are -- of course, the other question that was asked was on unbilled. Unbilled is a matter that happens towards the end of a quarter and so on. We are not unduly concerned with that for the simple reason that all of them have been scrutinized extensively, thoroughly and there is no issue as far as that is concerned. But if I were to take days outstanding in terms of our receivable as well as UBR, it was 97 in this quarter as against 100 in the last quarter. So we have done quite well. ANKUR RUDRA: Fair enough. Congrats on the quarter and best of luck for the remaining quarters.

Thanks. KEDAR SHIRALI: Next question, Melissa. ANKUR RUDRA: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Edward Caso from Wells Fargo Securities. Please go ahead. EDWARD CASO, ANALYST, WELLS FARGO SECURITIES: Hi. Good evening. Thank you for taking my call. Could you talk a little bit about your BFSI space which continues to do well. What are you seeing? Are you -- what areas in particular are those clients focused on? And I'm particularly curious as to the regulatory aspect, if you're starting to see some of that work. Thank you. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: See, from a BFSI point of view, we see a lot of effort going on in efficiency still. They are called as transformation, sometimes called as re-engineering initiatives by the financial institutions, whereby they are looking at systems which are globally efficient. For example, payment is a big area. Securities processing is a big area. So there are specific areas in which significant amount of investment is going on in helping the financial institutions to have efficient systems, both in terms of a single footprint, also in terms of some of the latest technologies coming in to make them real time. So that's one area we are seeing. And then we are seeing a very good growth for our products for custody, for corporate actions, for payments, securities processing and also core banking. All these areas are getting a lot of traction across the board. And we are beginning to see infrastructure optimization deals in the financial institutions. Even though our infrastructure business has been growing significantly, we have done most of the transformation deals in the infrastructure space outside of the financial institutions. But we are seeing traction for those kind of deals as well. And in terms of compliance there is a lot of discussion going forward on the spend towards compliance. And we are working with a few banks on compliance initiatives but I think more to come. EDWARD CASO: Okay. And in the United Kingdom area, the government cut back and put a lot of price pressure on all the providers

a year or so ago. Could you give us an update on what that market is like, the UK government market? Thank you. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: See, in the UK government we are doing two or three very major engagements. I'm happy to report the prestigious engagement which is called the PADA initiative that is the pensions initiative, is on target and went operational last quarter. And the policies are already on the system and it's getting serviced and we are on track, both in terms of revenues and margins. We have not had an impact. The contract has gone through the government scrutiny and I'm happy to say that we are on track. There's no issues. EDWARD CASO: Right. Thank you and congratulations. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Moshe Katri from Cowen and Company. Please go ahead. MOSHE KATRI, ANALYST, COWEN AND COMPANY: Thanks. This is a question for Chandra. Any feedback or feedback on any (technical difficulty) discussions you're having with clients regarding calendar year 2012 IT budgets, and also maybe your views on when budgets will be finalized for next year? Thanks. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I have just recently met with a large number of the customers. And while I can make comments about what I've heard, I would like to wait 'til the December quarter to confirm everything, or sometime in November/December we can give you comments through informal interactions. But at this point in time, based on what I have heard, the budget cycle is going to be on target. There is not going to be any delays. For most of the clients I have spoken to, they have said they are going through their budget cycles as per plan. And there will be increases in budgets in some situations. There will be same budget in some other situations. I have not heard of significant reduction in budgets. So at this point in time we look positive. We are expecting the budgets to get finalized by the end of the calendar year. But, you know, we just have to be watchful.

MOSHE KATRI: Well, that's good news. And then -- N. CHANDRASEKARAN: We are looking for any specific negative news that may come. We are scrutinizing any delays, any project cancellations, or any drop in budgets, or any shrinkage of revenue targets from our teams. We are looking at all of those things very carefully to see if there is a pattern. And we will continue to watch for -- MOSHE KATRI: I have one last question. On recruiting it looks like your recruiting (technical difficulty) on a six to 12-months basis. Is the recruiting proceeding as planned or is the pace of recruiting slowing a bit given the macro uncertainty. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I couldn't hear your question. Are you asking is our pace of recruitment slowing down? MOSHE KATRI: I'm asking, looking at your recruiting plans from a six to 12-months perspective going forward, is the ramp on the recruiting side proceeding as originally planned, or is the pace of recruiting slowing a bit given macro uncertainties. AJOY MUKHERJEE: As far our recruitment is concerned, we are on target. We announced 60,000 this year and we are sticking to that as far as this financial year is concerned. And for the next financial year we will do the planning when the time comes and then let you know. MOSHE KATRI: Understood. Thank you very much. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Joseph Foresi from Janney Montgomery Scott. Please go ahead. JOSEPH FORESI, ANALYST, JANNEY MONTGOMERY SCOTT: Hi. I wonder if I could just follow up on the 2012 question. I wonder, in your discussions with your clients, is it too early to tell what 2012 is going to bring in? If you could just put it in context with how your discussions this year have matched up with other years. I was wondering just if you could give us a little color on that side. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Yes, it is too early to be giving you a very firm picture on 2012. But having said that, the recent meetings with customers and their outlook on

IT spending, outlook on projects, on discretionary spend, etc., I don't see any change in direction from customers. And I think that they're all working towards doing a normal cycle for their budgets. That's what I'm hearing. But we need to see as we go closer into November, and end of November, early December will be the time to take a firm view. JOSEPH FORESI: And then just following up on that, is the tone the same across all your client base? And maybe you could just talk about how this -- entering 2012 feels compared to what you might have experienced in past years, maybe even 2009, just from a total spend perspective. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Surprisingly, I would say that the mood across sectors, manufacturing, retail, financial services, the utilities sectors, all match up and they seem to be positive. And going into the year at this point in time, definitely it is a lot better than the 2008, 2009 time frame. But having said that, every single day, if you read a newspaper or if you watch TV, you get very worried. JOSEPH FORESI: Just one last one. Just, you said it's better than the 2008/2009 time frame. Is that because the type of spend is different? Is it more maintenance work versus discretionary? I'm just trying to get a sense of why, besides the fact that things were much worse back then, why you're feeling a little bit more comfortable outside of what the clients are telling you. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I think one is the body language because the customers, their attitude is that we are not expecting a sudden recovery in the US or Europe. We know that the recovery or the employment situation is going to take considerably long time. And they also are aware of the sovereign debt crisis. All the things that we worry about, we hear multiple different opinions about, according to them is no new data point. All these are data points that are known. So they are going about spending their money in two ways. Definitely getting more and more

efficient from their IT platform. And then they all want to grow so -- they are very careful in where they are spending, but they are spending. They are extremely careful. It's not that the CapEx is going to be just made available easily. There's a lot of scrutiny. But there is a method by which they are going about it. That's why I feel better. JOSEPH FORESI: Thank you. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Mitali Ghosh from Bank of America - Merrill Lynch. Please go ahead. MITALI GHOSH, MERRILL LYNCH - ANALYST, BANK OF AMERICA: Yes, thanks. Congratulations on the good volume growth in the quarter. Just wanted to understand that one often hears of customers wanting to squeeze their maintenance spend in order to fund the discretionary part of the spend. What does that imply in terms of realization for you? And how are you really approaching that? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Mitali, I think that will always be there. Definitely in some companies there is an additional budget. In some companies the budget may stay the same, so they may try to change the mix of run versus change so they can get more of the programs in. Those discussions will have to be had. So in those situations you've got to specifically work on all the levers that you have. Are you offering a fixed price, are you offering more offshore leverage or are you cutting down functionality or are you consolidating applications, are you rationalizing them. Or can we do a maintenance contract with a transformation contract bundle. So there are so many different scenarios and so many different options exercised by customers. So I can't paint a broad brush picture on that. But we are very focused on our gross margins, I would say. And it's very difficult to get price increase at this point, at least in near term. So we are extremely focused on margins and especially gross margins and we will continue to drive that. MITALI GHOSH: Right, so overall, what should one expect from a realization

trend point of view? And just linked to that, Maha, in terms of the margin guidance for the year, any update on that in terms of where you think can hold the margin, given the rupee depreciation. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: On the first point, I think you should expect that the pricing will be stable or continue on normal course. There won't be any big jumps or big drops. And there may be a marginal increase in a quarter, there may be a marginal - a few tens of basis points drop in another quarter. So it will more or less be stable. And, Maha -- S. MAHALINGAM: So, you know, Mitali, as you know, we don't give any guidance. I've not given any guidance as far as the margin is concerned. But certainly we have talked about an aspirational goal of 27% which we believe the cost structure certainly enables us to give that. And thus we believe we should be in a position to -- we should be able to reach that. Now you see the -- as you know, it's a factor of growth and, of course, of reining in of expenditure and cost controls and so on. Somewhere, as I have always maintained, around INR46 or thereabouts we should be in a position to see this kind of margin. We do not see any roadblock coming in the way. MITALI GHOSH: And given that the rupee is today trading at about INR46, so should one expect that you could do better than that level or would you be really looking to invest it back in the business? S. MAHALINGAM: I think rupee is trading at INR49. You said INR46, but it is not. It's trading at INR49. MITALI GHOSH: Yes, I said about INR46. S. MAHALINGAM: About INR46. You see, the basic thing is our investment strategies are driven regardless of the -- as we have always said, we continue to recruit. We believe there is a demand and we can see the demand. There has not been any slackening of demand, as we have given commentary

so far. So that would continue to go on. So I don't think I am going to -- we are going to invest especially just because we have an uptick from a currency perspective. MITALI GHOSH: Right. And just lastly if I can check with Ajoy in terms of the campus hiring. What are the kind of offers that you have made? And are salary levels being offered flat versus last year. AJOY MUKHERJEE: Yes, Mitali. The salary levels are same as what we offered last year. And our campus recruitment process is in full swing at this point in time. It will continue next quarter as well. So far we have given 35,000 offers already for them to join us next fiscal. MITALI GHOSH: Okay. Thank you very much. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Diviya Nagarajan from UBS. Please go ahead. DIVIYA NAGARAJAN, ANALYST, UBS: Hi. Congrats on the quarter. A couple of questions. I noticed for the last few quarters our top ten clients seem to have slowed down in relation to the Company average. This quarter in particular your top client and your top two to five clients have grown sub 2%. Is there anything that we should read into this? Is it is because these clients are maturing? And do you expect they will continue to grow slower than the rest of the Company in the future as well? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I think there's nothing wrong with the top clients or the top five clients. They are excellent clients, with repeat business and absolute amount, they are growing. They are continuing to grow, continuing to present opportunities. But beyond a size, they may not grow at the same average as the Company. In one quarter they may be low, another quarter they may be the same. But in absolute amount they are growing. DIVIYA NAGARAJAN: Got that. And I notice that in our earlier commentary we said that price realizations were down about almost 2% this quarter. Even last quarter I think price realizations were a bit down. And when

I'm trying to reconcile this with our commentary that pricing is stable, but we've actually seen two quarters of pricing realization dips, do you expect this to stabilize from current levels? Or do you think that as your mix changes, you could see this realization dip, even though the absolute like-to-like pricing might continue to remain flat? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Pricing per se has not dipped actually. Most of the things that you see is a combination of service mix and market mix I think. Last quarter we had a significant impact due to the market mix. This quarter, to some extent, it is the service mix, applications doing low and some of the other services doing more. So that's the primary reason. There is no -- we are not losing any price on any of the deals. And I wouldn't say that that should be a cause of concern. DIVIYA NAGARAJAN: Sure. Just one last question and maybe I'll come for a follow-up after that. Your peer Infosys which reported last week talked about an evenly spread momentum, revenue momentum through the year. We typically see a seasonality, with December being a little weak because of holiday seasons. Do you subscribe to the view this is likely to be an evenly spaced out year or do you expect normal seasonality to prevail for your Company? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: From a TCS point of view, we had an excellent growth in Q1. Our volumes in Q1 and Q2 have been spectacular. On the back of a solid volume performance in Q1, once again we have delivered a 6.3% volume growth this quarter. And the momentum is good. And in the third quarter you always have the issues of Thanksgiving holidays, Christmas holidays and the number of working days you have and Diwali holidays in India. So all those factors play a role. And customers, going into December, the decision-making will probably slow down. So you may not have ramp-ups suddenly happening in the month of December. So those things always will present themselves. And the other thing that happens in December is

the manufacturing and the hi-tech electronics companies will always have what we call as a furlough. There will be shutdowns one week, two weeks, especially hi-tech companies and the manufacturing companies. This has become a norm over the last three, four years. So from a Q3 point of view I would say the holidays plus this will be issue. But apart from that I think the momentum is good. So you should factor that. From a TCS point of view you should factor that when you make estimates. DIVIYA NAGARAJAN: Thanks, that was helpful. And all the best for the rest of the year. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Sandeep Shah from RBS. Please go ahead. SANDEEP SHAH, ANALYST, RBS: Yes, sir. Is it possible to give some color on the volume growth within the International business? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Mr. Sandeep, all the growth has come from International. We have had a 5% revenue down in Domestic business this quarter and then our LatAm business has been pretty muted. So if our revenues are 9% and our volume growth for the overall is 6.3%, means it will be somewhere very close to the International revenue growth. SANDEEP SHAH: And sir, what is the reason in terms of a significant decline in our fixed price revenue contribution in this quarter? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: See the fixed price is -- always some projects get ended and some projects get started. You also have to see the fact that for the last year, year and a half, you have always had issues in large-scale fixed-price signings. We have not had large-scale fixed-price signings for some quarters, so some of those projects are running -- are getting over. So we will be signing new contracts. The pipeline is good, so it should go up. We have always said that (technical difficulty) hold us to 48 or 49 or 50. The fixed price portion will always oscillate between 40 to 50. That's the range we have given and we have, for a period of time, always been operating 47 to

49. And this quarter it has come down (technical difficulty). SANDEEP SHAH: Okay. Sir, the closure of these fixed projects may have some impact in the Q3 or already we have the new deals which would be sufficient to compensate? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: It depends on how much you are projecting. SANDEEP SHAH: Okay. And sir, just bookkeeping questions. It seems this quarter the short-term borrowings has gone up significantly. What is the reason for the same? And if I'm not wrong the invested cash seems down on a Q-on-Q basis from INR105b to almost like INR95b. S. MAHALINGAM: See as far as the short-term borrowing is concerned this is more a question of management of funds. We wanted to make sure that the -- that we don't break deposits (technical difficulty) management area. And as far as investments are concerned, we haven't seen any decline as far as this quarter is concerned. SANDEEP SHAH: Okay. Sir, if I'm not wrong, last quarter it was INR105b. S. MAHALINGAM: Yes. SANDEEP SHAH: So in the opening remarks you said INR95b despite we have a borrowing which has increased in this quarter. S. MAHALINGAM: No, the main thing is that you'll have to take account the dividends that we have paid as well as we have paid something like INR2,500 crores in terms of dividend during -- because there were two dividends, our final dividend for last year which was done after the AGM and then the second one was the first quarter interim dividend. And on top of it we have also paid for the acquisition of certain Diligenta balance shares and so on. So both of them, put together, have resulted in money outflow. SANDEEP SHAH: Okay. And just Maha, a last question in terms of the OCI balances related to the ForEx as in outstanding in the balance sheet. S. MAHALINGAM: So OCI we have got INR528 crores in OCI. And as you know it really depends on how the rupee factors in for it to translate into the right trend of loss or profit or whatever it is. SANDEEP

SHAH: Okay. So this is a positive or a negative number? S. MAHALINGAM: This is a negative one at this time. SANDEEP SHAH: Okay. And what is the Q-on-Q movement here? S. MAHALINGAM: Q-on-Q movement, there has been a significant jump as far as the OCI is concerned, mainly because of a huge swing in volatility in currency rates. SANDEEP SHAH: Okay. Thanks and all the best. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Vihang Naik from MF Global. Please go ahead. VIHANG NAIK, ANALYST, MF GLOBAL: Hi. Congratulations on a good set of numbers. Actually all of my questions are answered, thanks. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Pinku Pappan from Nomura. Please go ahead. PINKU PAPPAN, ANALYST, NOMURA: Hi, thanks for taking the questions. Chandra, could you just talk about telecom. Is it something particular to a particular client or is something secular you're seeing, why there was a dip in this quarter? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: See, telecom has been not performing well for a long time, except the last quarter. For about four or five quarters, we have had issues and last quarter we did well. And then this quarter again we have not done well, because there has not been a consistent performance from varying accounts in telecom for some time. But as I said, I'm quite happy that we have signed actually three deals. One we announced towards the end of last quarter and two we announced this quarter. All three are really large strategic customers for us with a huge potential to grow. And we are also pursuing some deals in the telecom sector. I am so glad that again the deal pipeline in telecom has picked up. So I think we should do better from here, but this has been a troubled sector for some time. PINKU PAPPAN: Okay. And you talked about chasing 10 large deals. Can you just give a profile of where these deals are, in the industry segments? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Industry segment-wise they are pretty well rounded and we are chasing

across all the industries; banking, insurance, retail, energy sector, pharma sector. So it's pretty distributed. And in terms of markets they are predominantly in the North America and Europe, at least four and four and then the two deals are outside. Well-qualified good deals are being pursued. PINKU PAPPAN: And finally, you talked about not finding any signs of cutback or cancellation of projects. Specific to Continental Europe what are you hearing? Is there any cause for concern there, any red flags that we can potentially hear from that region? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: See, we have delivered almost a 10% growth this quarter on back of double-digit growth in Continental Europe last quarter. So I think our pipeline in Continental Europe is good; our incremental revenues are coming in. But again as you know from a macro point of view it is one of the most troubled regions. So we just need to see what happens to Greece, what happens to the euro zone, what kind of decisions are being taken, what are the institutions that get affected. We just have to watch. So we are looking for any possible signs of negative news, so that we can be prepared on the ground to tackle it. PINKU PAPPAN: Okay, thank you very much. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Sandip Agarwal from Antique Stockbroking. Please go ahead. SANDIP AGARWAL, ANALYST, ANTIQUE STOCKBROKING: Hi, good evening, sir, Sandip here. I have just a couple of questions. One was on the tax. Where do you see the full year tax for FY'12 to go and what kind of CapEx you are expecting for FY'12 and FY'13? If you can throw some light, that will be great. And thirdly, on the utilization side, now your attrition has come down significantly and you have added a lot of capacity as freshers, so probably one quarter or maybe two quarters there could some hit in your utilization, but otherwise I expect it to go up. So am I right in my expectation or what? S. MAHALINGAM: The first question on this

tax rate, we are essentially somewhere at -- we are at 24% and that is more or less what we are going to have for this year. It becomes a little difficult to say exactly. I have been talking about 23%, 24%. It depends on the extent of non-SEZ revenues that we have and all that kind of thing. So I think it will be good to factor in about 24%. And next year we will make the announcements later as to what the tax rate is going to be. But I've always maintained that around 25% we'll reach and thereafter come down. But whether it is going to be a little over 25% and so on I'll make that announcement a little later. As far as the CapEx for '12/'13 is concerned, we have not arrived at it now and we are not making any announcement as yet. As far as utilization is concerned, Ajoy will -- AJOY MUKHERJEE: As far as utilization is concerned if you look at last four to six quarters, we have been operating at 82% to 84% in between. And we are quite confident of operating at this higher end of utilization, sustaining that. SANDIP AGARWAL: Sir, thank you. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Nitin Mohta from Macquarie. Please go ahead. NITIN MOHTA, ANALYST, MACQUARIE: Thank you. My question essentially relates to if you look at the current demand structure and given your feedback, you will obviously have multiple margin tailwinds in terms of currency depreciation as well as attrition is coming down and there does not seem to be a supply side of pressure. So if you can please outline areas where we would like to reap the benefits that you get from these. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: But you know you also have to look at the kind of investments we are making. Even in this quarter if you see, on the back of a huge intake last quarter, it's again a huge intake this quarter. Last quarter also we had a significant intake of trainees and all

their salaries come for the full quarter this quarter. On top of that we have taken 20,000 plus this quarter. So it's a huge employee expense. So that's the kind of investment that is needed in order to be ready for capturing the growth. And also we will continue to invest more in intellectual property in our product platforms and frameworks as we've been investing. We have a product business where we are continuing to increase investments; it's doing well. iON, our small- and medium-business platform we will continue to invest. And we are looking at a couple of more platform plays so we'll look at that. So there will be investments for which we will need to be building margin cushion. NITIN MOHTA: Thanks. And then if I can have a follow up. In terms of utilization, it's been obviously a couple of quarters where you guys have been operating at an industry-high utilization level. In your client conversations does that come up as compared to your preparedness to service them vis-a-vis your peers who are operating at a lower utilization level? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: See I think the way to look at it is that with 83.1% utilization we have been delivering incredible volume growth. And that is not possible if we cannot service and execute. So we have demonstrated that at this utilization level, because of our size, we can bring this size and scale into play and deliver to the requirements of our clients. NITIN MOHTA: Thank you and all the best for the remaining quarters. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Thank you. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Aarti Mishra from CLSA. Miss Aarti Mishra please go ahead, your line has been un-muted. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Go to the next one. KEDAR SHIRALI: Yes, you can take the next question, Melissa. OPERATOR: Sure. The next question is from Ritesh Rathod from UTI Mutual Fund. Please go ahead. RITESH RATHOD, ANALYST, UTI MUTUAL FUND: Yes, hello, sir. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Hello. RITESH RATHOD: Yes, sir, can you hear me? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Yes, I can hear you. RITESH

RATHOD: Okay. Just on the variable pay, how much we paid in this quarter? Can you just highlight? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: 100% payout. RITESH RATHOD: And what was last quarter, the same number? S. MAHALINGAM: Yes, more or less the same number. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: See the only variation is that at the senior levels we will link it to performance. Some will get more; some will get less. But basically we are paying out the 100%. RITESH RATHOD: And in terms of loans and advances, the other assets which has increased, is there any higher -- like what is the outstanding amount to the Tata Group companies, which we have advanced. There was an added increase from the last balance sheet number which we disclosed. S. MAHALINGAM: No, there has been no increase from the last balance sheet number that we disclosed in terms of what we have given to Tata Group. It is not a very significant amount. RITESH RATHOD: Okay, thank you. That's all from my side. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of [Pranav Tendulkar] from IDBI Mutual Fund. Please go ahead. PRANAV TENDULKAR, ANALYST, IDBI MUTUAL FUND: Yes. First of all, do you see European demand coming down or what is the characteristic of Europe demand because you had an excellent quarter in terms of Europe volume growth or revenue growth? And second thing is what kind of traction you are having on the SMB platform that we launched. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: On the Europe side, I think we have delivered two continuous quarters of good growth. And we have not seen any specific letup at this point of time. On the SMB side, the platform is doing very well and a number of -- over 250 customers have gone live and deal signings have crossed 400. But it is taking time to make the customers operational because of the nature of the current setup they have. But I think the live customers is increasing every day and the rollouts are happening every day. I think we should pick up scale in the coming

quarters. PRANAV TENDULKAR: Okay, thanks a lot. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Thank you. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Yogesh Aggarwal from HSBC. Please go ahead. YOGESH AGGARWAL, ANALYST, HSBC: Yes, hi. Just have a couple of clarifications. Maha, the cross currency impact on the top line is 50 basis points, right for the quarter? S. MAHALINGAM: Yes, I think --- yes, it is 50 basis points. It is 4.7 and 5.2. YOGESH AGGARWAL: Okay. And Chandra, just a small thing on the realization. You said it's because of the service mix. Is ADM billings usually more than Enterprise Solutions or the Products, etc. because if I see only BPO has grown or IMS might explain a little bit. But still you have some services which are higher billing services. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I think -- see there are -- Yogesh, I'm sorry I'm not able to give you a very straightforward answer because it's a little complex. Although the time and material billing has gone up and fixed price has come down, some fixed price contracts typically have higher realization than time and material. So, so many factors are playing. It is not that [assurance] is lower pricing than ADM. So that's the wrong conclusion to make. So there are three or four factors playing in, (technical difficulty), time and materials, service mix, when the job has been, all of them are playing in and that is why you are seeing this volatility. YOGESH AGGARWAL: Okay. But it's safe to assume flat from here, realizations? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: It is. It is very safe to assume that. YOGESH AGGARWAL: Okay, thanks a lot. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Priya Sunder from Avendus Securities. Please go ahead. PRIYA SUNDER, ANALYST, AVENDUS SECURITIES: Yes, hi. My question is specific to the BFSI segment. As I gather from industry commentary that a lot of contracts are coming up for renewal in this particular vertical compared to what it was three years back. What I've been hearing is usually in a year you get 5%, 6%

of contracts coming up for renewal. Over the next one or two years you'll have at least 25%, 30%. So given that BFSI is your largest vertical, how do you think you're placed to tackle this and will you be seeing a significant erosion in pricing in such a case? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: As you yourself pointed out it is a sector in which we are very strong, so we think that we are well positioned if there is such contracts that are coming up. And I don't see a drop in pricing. PRIYA SUNDER: Okay. You also mentioned that discretionary spending is turning towards emerging areas like cloud, the big data technology and all. Can you elaborate on what kind of services you currently offer in these areas and what's your strategy going forward? What kind of revenue contribution do you see -- do you have currently and probably over the next five years from these new areas? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I'm sorry I have to make a big presentation to you on this. It can't be answered in one line. But having said that, we have significant investments in these areas like social media, mobility, listening platforms, big data, (inaudible). There are centers of excellence, there are large teams both in the US, in the Valley as well as in India and in our local delivery centers in the US markets. And we work with a number of customers. We have done more than 100 engagements in these technologies and we are winning more engagements. So I think we are in a phase that we are working with customers to redefine what is possible using these technologies. So it takes time to build scale. But I can't give you revenue projections at this point. PRIYA SUNDER: Okay, thanks. That's it from me. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Rahul Jain from Dolat Capital. Please go ahead. RAHUL JAIN, ANALYST, DOLAT CAPITAL: Yes, just an extension of an earlier question about Europe. It's one of the most uncertain in terms and the kind of negative news

flow we have from the region. But this is one of the most performing geography for us. What do we see as the key factor driving this performance? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: It's very difficult to say. One is see you can look at it in multiple different ways. You can look at it the way that our revenues currently are [very]small , you can look at it that way. The second way you can look at it is that companies are now more open to the kind of services that we are able to offer to make them more efficient. You can read it like that. And the third is that our presence in these markets and the sales have increased. There are multiple different dimensions in which you can interpret. But the point is that the macro and corporate world, there is a disconnect. RAHUL JAIN: Okay. And can we even consider the point wherein the incumbent European players do not have a much well-shaped outsourcing model as compared to a certain extent US players have done, but European players have not been able to create a outsourcing model? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I would like to avoid making comments on competition. I would like to stick with our results please. RAHUL JAIN: Okay. And another -- just another question. LatAm specifically has been seeing a sort of a flat revenue over the last several quarters. So do we have some specific way to propel this or is this going to be flat for some more quarters? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I think LatAm has been volatile. That has been the problem. There've been -- some markets have been growing, some markets have been falling. But they have grown this quarter and I expect to see growth in the coming quarters. RAHUL JAIN: Okay, that's it from my side. Thanks a lot. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Aviral Gupta from Indiabulls Mutual Fund. Please go ahead. AVIRAL GUPTA, ANALYST, INDIABULLS MUTUAL FUND: Good evening, sir. My questions regarding the realization have been answered. Thank you. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Yes,

next question. KEDAR SHIRALI: Next question, Melissa. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Shweta Malik from Marwadi Shares and Finance Limited. Please go ahead. SHWETA MALIK, ANALYST, MARWADI SHARES & FINANCE LIMITED: Yes, thanks. All my questions have been answered. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Radhika Merwin from PUG Securities. Please go ahead. RADHIKA MERWIN, ANALYST, PUG SECURITIES: Sir, congrats on a very good set of numbers. A couple of things. One, when you had mentioned earlier on the realization card that it was a mix of change in service mix and also your time and material and fixed. So specific to that, there has been a significant change in your time and material versus your fixed price contracts this particular quarter. So is there anything particular that has happened this particular quarter, because almost 300 basis points is the difference that is showing on a sequential basis. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: No, nothing to read except that some of the large fixed price contracts we signed before have come to an end and that has affected the portion of fixed price versus time and material. And this also reflects that there was a -- a few quarters back there was a slack in the deal signings in fixed price contracts, which are predominantly discretionary fund related. So I think that's getting corrected, so it should go back up. But we've always said that you should be comfortable to see this number between 40% and 50%. RADHIKA MERWIN: All right. So in terms of the deal wins that we have signed this particular quarter, we are back on track in terms of deal wins on the fixed-price contract having seen that come back this particular quarter with the robust deal signing. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: No, some we have signed, but I can't really tell you next quarter whether we will back at the same percentage as the previous. I can't say that. But it will slowly inch forward. RADHIKA MERWIN: Right. And just one last -- just a color on FY'13. I

know people have asked this before. But just on a general understanding and since you're interacting with your clients, is there something that you would view differently right now for the next year vis-a-vis how you would be probably six months ago, because you're still going strong on hiring? But are you viewing FY'13 with a little bit more skepticism particularly to Europe I mean? Do you see some particular concern that might relook at FY'13 numbers? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: See at this point in time, based on what we have heard, we are creating the capacity for growth. And even this quarter we have signed four important deals in Europe. So I think we have to go by data; we can't go by hunch and market sentiment. We go by data. If we go by data, we have to prepare for growth. RADHIKA MERWIN: Sure. And the ForEx loss this particular quarter was how much exactly, sir, out of the other income? S. MAHALINGAM: INR91 crores. RADHIKA MERWIN: INR91 crores was the ForEx loss, okay. And we will continue maintaining our hedge position for the duration that we maintained. Are we changing? Are we going more short in terms of hedge positions or are we going to maintain it at the same level? S. MAHALINGAM: See at the current time, we have got a set of measures. We have a Risk Management Board meeting which happens regularly to keep reviewing it. I can't comment on whether we'll continue to maintain it and so on. But certainly our outlook at this moment in time is to look at the short term, six months and then we can -- we are continuing with that. But beyond that I cannot say anything. RADHIKA MERWIN: Sure, okay. Thanks a lot, sir, and all the best. OPERATOR: Thank you. Ladies and gentlemen, that was the last question. I would now like to hand the floor back to Mr. Shirali and the management for closing comments. Please go ahead. KEDAR SHIRALI: Chandra, may I request you to give some closing remarks. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Thank you all

for joining us for the Q2 earnings announcement. As I said, we are happy with the results that we have declared. Particularly happy that we have been able to deliver the volume growth and the International revenue growth and from a sector point of view, happy to see BFSI and a number of verticals doing well. Happy with the deal signing in telecom and happy that the major markets including Europe have delivered. Rather disappointed that the volatility of the Indian market continues, which we are paying attention to. And our hiring is on track. And going by -- even though there is a lot of macro nervousness and about unemployment in the US and about the debt crisis -- going by the data points that we have based on on-the-ground meetings with customers, we think there is a very good opportunity. And we will continue to prepare ourselves while at the same time being very careful in listening to any signs that we can pick up. If we pick up any negative signs, we will be the first ones to come back to you and tell you there is something going on. Otherwise we will prepare for growth and thank you all for your support. OPERATOR: Thank you, gentlemen of the management. Ladies and gentlemen, on behalf of TCS that gentlemen of the management. Ladies and gentlemen, on behalf of TCS that concludes this conference call. Thank you for joining us and you may now disconnect your lines. Thank you. Q2 2011 TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD Earnings Conference Call - Final https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/q2-2011-tata-consultancy-services-ltd-earnings/docview/762380993/se-2?accountid=27562 Abstract: None available. Full text: OPERATOR: Ladies and gentlemen, good day and welcome to the TCS earnings conference call. Please note that, for the duration of this presentation, all participant lines will be in a listen-only mode. Please note that this conference is being recorded. After the presentation, there will be an opportunity for you to ask questions. (Operator Instructions). At this time, I would like to turn the conference over to Mr. Kedar Shirali, Director Investor Relations at TCS. Thank you, and over to you, Mr. Shirali. KEDAR SHIRALI, DIRECTOR, IR, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Thank you, Melissa. And good evening and welcome, everyone. Thank you for joining us today to discuss TCS's financial results for the second quarter of fiscal year 2011 that ended September 30, 2010. This call is being webcast through our website and an archive, including the transcript, will be available on the site, for the duration of this quarter. The financial statements, results presentation and press releases are also available on our website. Our leadership team is present on this call to discuss our results. We have with us today, Mr. N. Chandrasekaran, Chief Executive Officer and Managing Director. N. CHANDRASEKARAN, CEO AND MD, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Hello. Good morning, good afternoon and good evening, everyone. KEDAR SHIRALI: Mr. S. Mahalingam, Chief Financial Officer and Executive Director. S. MAHALINGAM, CFO AND EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Hello. KEDAR SHIRALI: Mr. Phiroz A. Vandrevala, Head of Global Corporate Affairs and Executive Director. PHIROZ VANDREVALA, HEAD OF GLOBAL CORPORATE AFFAIRS AND EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Good day, everybody. KEDAR SHIRALI: And Mr. Ajoyendra Mukherjee, Head of Global Human Resources. AJOYENDRA MUKHERJEE, HEAD OF GLOBAL HR, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Hello. KEDAR SHIRALI: As usual, Chandra and Maha will give us a brief overview of the Company's performance, and that will be followed by the Q&A session. As you are aware, we do not provide specific revenue and earnings guidance. Anything said on this call which reflects our outlook for the

future, or which can be construed as a forward-looking statement, must be reviewed in conjunction with the risks that the Company faces. We have outlined these risks in the second slide of the results presentation emailed to you, and it's also made available on our website. With that, I'd like to turn the call over to Chandra, to begin the proceedings. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Thank you, Kedar. Hello, friends. It's a pleasure to connect with all of you, to share the highlights of our Q2 results. In summary, it has been a very satisfying quarter and a quarter where we have achieved several milestones. And it's the first quarter where we cross $2b in revenue. And, more importantly, our incremental revenues for the quarter is $210m. And, in an environment which has been uncertain, not only we have been able to see the demand recovery, but also able to capture that demand and execute flawlessly. And the volumes have been about 11.2%, again, a very, very credible number. And, in terms of the US market, our revenues exceeded $1b this quarter, and all of our verticals or industry segments, have grown double-digit during the quarter. All the key markets, namely the Americas, Europe, India and Asia Pacific, have registered double-digit growth. So, it has been a very well-spread-out performance. And then, in terms of operating margin, we have done very well to improve our margins by 86 basis points and deliver growth at the operating margin level as well as at the net margin level higher than the revenue level. On a year-on-year basis, the growth has been 23% (sic -- presentation) on the revenue line and about 32% on the net profit line. To talk about the deals, I think the pipeline has been very good and, as I say, it has been well-rounded. And we closed the eight large deals, two in banking, two in retail, one each in insurance, life sciences, garment and media sectors. In terms of our markets, half of those deals came from the US and the remaining deals came each from different markets. In

terms of services, again it has been an excellent quarter, because most of our services have grown double-digit or near double-digit. Infrastructure services has seen an excellent demand and a great growth. And the Enterprise Solutions, which signals the recovery of the discretionary spend has grown excellently well, more than the Company growth rate. In terms of employees, again, our HR team has done a fantastic job of recruiting the highest ever gross addition, 19,293 employees. And, even on a net basis, it is the highest ever addition all done organically. Our pipeline looks very robust. And, barring the macro uncertainty that prevails, which we have to be watchful about, we are very positive and bullish about the demand environment and are preparing ourselves for the future. Those will be my initial comments. Now I'll turn it over to Maha, and then later on we can take questions. S. MAHALINGAM: Thanks, Chandra. Here are the headline numbers, once again. We posted revenues of INR92.864b, which is a growth of 13% Q-on-Q and 24.9% year-on-year. In constant currency, the sequential growth was 11%. In dollar terms, the reported revenue is $2.004b, which is a sequential growth of 11.7% and Y-on-Y growth of 30.3%. The breakup of quarterly revenue growth in INR terms is as follows; volume growth, plus 11.2%; exchange, plus 205 basis points; pricing, minus 26 basis points; total, 13%. From a margin perspective, we had a significant headwind in September quarter from the promotions given in July. On the other hand, we benefited from a quarter-on-quarter currency movement, high utilization and greater operational leverage arising out of our continued cost management in the face of good top line growth. And, therefore, we are able -- we were able to expand the operating margin by 86 basis points quarter on quarter, to 28%. The impact of different levers on the operating margin is as follows; currency, 103 basis points; impact of promotions and the variable payout -- additional variable payout, negative 166 basis points; rate productivity improvement, 95 basis points; SG&A efficiencies, 54 basis points positive. So, totally, 86 basis

points. Other income was lower this quarter due to net foreign exchange loss of INR537m and the reduced cash balance after last year's special and other dividend payouts. Our effective tax rate was 18.95%, giving us a net income of INR21,065m (sic -- see presentation). That's a net margin of 22.68%, a 23 basis points expansion Q-on-Q. Lastly, our DSO for the quarter was 78 days. Invested funds stood at INR71.7b. To conclude, we have had an excellent quarter, posting double-digit sequential growth, even in a difficult global economic environment. Importantly, we showed that, with customer focus, disciplined selling, full services capability and good execution, it's possible to achieve such high growth without compromising on profitability or, for that matter, cash flows. And this is what industry leadership is all about. With that, we can open the line for questions. OPERATOR: Thank you, sir. Ladies and gentlemen, we will now begin with the question and answer session. (Operator Instructions). The first question is from the line of Joseph Foresi from Janney Montgomery Scott. Please go ahead. JOSEPH FORESI, ANALYST, JANNEY MONTGOMERY SCOTT: Hi, gentlemen, and congratulations on some nice results here. My first question is just on demand. Maybe you could just describe to us what's going on, in a little bit more detail. Is this a catch up? I mean, obviously, the hiring numbers would lead us to believe that you're expecting demand to be strong for a couple of quarters, going forward. What are you seeing in the environment, right now? And just what's driving the growth and the strength of it? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I don't think that it is pent-up demand. The way I would like to describe is that, number one, we have made investments in different markets and different industries. All of them are picking up momentum and that is driving growth, at one level. But, at our large customers segment, there are two things that we are seeing. One, every business is extremely focused on becoming efficient which means consolidation of multiple ERPs. If they are having multiple core banking systems going to a

single instance; or if they have done a lot of acquisitions in the past, wanting to bring it all together; or in the infrastructure space, if they had too many data centers, if they had too many servers, utilization, consolidation, a transformation initiative. So, a number of things that actually is related to bringing efficiencies. Everyone is focused on that. The second trend is every business is definitely wanting to grow. And every business we talk to in our customer segment has got a growth agenda. It's called by different names. Some are focused on getting customer insights. Some are focused on introducing new products faster. Some are achieving growth through geographic expansion. So, there is an initiative, there is a focus, and all of this is resulting in picking up of new projects and new initiatives. And -- so we are seeing opportunities on both sides. We have had a -- preliminary discussions with some of our customers. And, while the full budget cycle is not complete, and we will have more data towards the end of the quarter, on a very small sample of dialogs that I have had, I think we're going to see increase in spending next fiscal year. JOSEPH FORESI: Okay. And then just, secondly, building on that, with attrition rates tweaking up and the rupee appreciating here, the positive side is obviously demand is very strong. What would be your primary concern heading into next year? Is it the labor supply? Is it the movement in currency? What are you monitoring to just make sure that things stay as good as they are? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I think three or four things. One is that the -- definitely, attrition is something that we would like to be brought down, and we are at about 14%. While, from a statistics point of view, we probably have the lowest attrition in the industry, but it's still a pretty large number and, when you translate it to absolute numbers, it's pretty large. So we are really kicking off a number of initiatives on employee engagement, and we are

focused on bringing it down. That's one area we really are concerned. The second area is definitely currency, and there is a -- definitely a headwind. And we were profitable at INR46 to $1, and it's come down already. So that's an area we'll continue to watch. The third thing is, we have been able to capture the demand very nicely, and our teams have done a superb job of execution. The recruitment, fulfillment, kicking off of the projects and executing those projects, all of this has worked together really nicely. As we scale, we need to continue to bring that engine to be [a giant]. We are already demonstrating our agility but to sustain that agility what are the tools that we can deploy, how we can really bring the organization to work together to capture the demand. That's something that we are to definitely watch out. And fourth is the regulatory measures that is being talked about in different parts of the world, the US in particular, and probably in some other markets. What kind of changes will come? And, again, we need to be watching that space and do things to adapt ourselves and matching this to our business model as we go along. So, these are probably the three or four things that we need to watch out for. JOSEPH FORESI: One last quick question. Europe did very well, have you seen a full recovery there? Or is that still late cycle? Thank you. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I don't think there's anything called a full recovery. As I said, the sentence that we are being watchful of the macroeconomic environment is going to be repeated probably for the foreseeable future. So we've said it in the past, and we're going to keep saying it for the next several quarters, because it's not going to be a quick recovery. But having said that, I think the demand side is very, very good and the pipeline is very strong, and we are very positive. And Europe in particular, if I have to comment, I think it has been slow.

It lags behind the US, and I think it will continue to lag behind. JOSEPH FORESI: Okay. Thank you. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Moshe Katri from Cowen & Company. Please go ahead. MOSHE KATRI, ANALYST, COWEN & COMPANY: Thanks, congratulations on a very strong quarter. Chandra, can you talk a bit about some of the underlying drivers for the demand for some of the larger verticals at TCS? And then perhaps you can talk a bit about the pricing dynamics and whether we're going to start seeing any improvements in pricing down the road? Thanks. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I think, as I said, we have -- we've seen the recovery coming over the last few quarters steadily. And we saw the recovery in the financial services and that leading the growth. And last quarter, where we delivered very good numbers, we said that the growth is more well-rounded. But I think this quarter is probably the real perfect quarter in terms of well-rounded growth because every single industry segment has grown double digit. And initially the broad themes is what I explained. We are seeing both the efficiency related and the discretionary related. If you talk about the financial services industry, it will be like getting a single customer view. Getting the customer insights. Whereas if I talk about a manufacturing industry, it will be more about getting a single instance CRP or doing a transformation by bringing ERP systems and the shop floor systems together. And if I look at retail, there are a lot of optimization initiatives, inventory optimization, pricing optimization, mark down pricing. Sourcing optimization, there are a number of things the retailers are doing. And then a lot of interest in getting consumer insights, and then see how to use the latest technologies like social networking. Those things have not kicked in yet, but those are things that the retailers are talking about. Also there is a broad interest in getting optimization of infrastructure, whether it is consolidating data centers, whether it is optimizing servers, virtualization. So that is across

industries primarily in retail, manufacturing, high tech industries. If you take pharma and banking there is a lot of compliance where it's ICD-10 compliance in pharma, there is Basel-related compliance, a number of compliance-led initiatives in banking and pharmaceutical companies. So I think the broad theme is efficiency and initiatives for growth. But when you go down to the industry and then do your segments, how we [consult] is different. And I gave you some examples. Hopefully that clarifies it. On the pricing environment, I had mentioned in the last call that we will probably see a pricing uptick end of this fiscal. The reason for that is we were seeing the demand environment improving and growth coming back. And certainly the volume growth for this quarter has been more than what we were anticipating about four, five months ago. And so the recovery has been pretty strong. And so when that happens, I think you should definitely see uptick in pricing. All of it may not reflect in the immediate quarter even if the pricing uptick comes in end of the fiscal, it's not going to reflect in Q1 pricing. But it'll have a lag effect but I'm fairly positive that it's going to be around that time, that we are going to see some pricing uptick. MOSHE KATRI: Just a final question for Maha. You're exiting this quarter with another record margin level. Can you talk a bit about some of the levers that are still in the model? And obviously the biggest risk here continues to be currency. Maybe you can go over some of the traditional lever points that you've mentioned in the past and give us a good feel on that? S. MAHALINGAM: From a revenue perspective, the levers that we have are the volume which has been delivered; the pricing, as Chandra said, is -- it's not factored into our calculation anywhere; and then of course the productivity improvement is something that we have -- we have pushed quite a lot in this quarter, it has manifested itself through the utilization. And that's --

we will continue to monitor that fairly carefully. Exchange is another factor which has worked in our favor in this quarter gain but of course as we go to the next quarter, currently rupee is at appreciated state, so there will be some issues with regard to that. So if I take the revenue drivers that I talked about, obviously the business momentum is reasonable. I'm not saying that this can be repeated and so on, but there are stirrings of a momentum. So that will hopefully be able to give us some sort of advantage. On the cost side, we have always emphasized, we don't want to cut down cost which will hurt us and therefore we are not doing anything with regard to that. But certainly the cost structure that we have could support this kind of revenue as well as hopefully somewhat higher. And therefore cost management to deliver higher revenues would always be a priority as we go along. These are essentially the cost drivers, and the currency headwind is something that we need to worry about at this point in time. But other than that, I think we are reasonably all right from a cost management perspective. MOSHE KATRI: Thanks, Maha. OPERATOR: Thank you. The next question is from the line of Ashwin Mehta from Nomura. Please go ahead. ASHWIN MEHTA, ANALYST, NOMURA: Hi, sir, congratulations on a good set of numbers. Two questions. One is in terms of our cash flow from operations. CHANDRASEKARAN: Essentially the pattern is not a very [serving] one because the --- basically, as far as accounts payable is concerned, it has remained at the same level. We are dealing with a higher accounts payable at this point in time. And therefore you see that operation. But the manner in which we monitor that, monitor this area is

to ensure that our collection comes as close to the billing as possible in a quarter. And therefore the accounts receivable is kept at a manageable level. And we have been able to do that, even with the growth perspective. So this the answer that I can give you because when you compare the first half of last year and the first half of this year, there are some differences. And as I said, it's not something that has not -- it's not a disturbing phenomenon. ASHWIN MEHTA: Okay. And secondly in terms of rentals on cost of revenue, that seems to have fallen off pretty sharply from almost 146 crores to almost near 79 crores. What's been the reason for that? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: There is some rationalization that has taken place in this quarter. But I'm not going to press this going forward. ASHWIN MEHTA: So it's -- N. CHANDRASEKARAN: (multiple speakers) go back to somewhat higher level. S. MAHALINGAM: It's a factor of multiple things, also, that which projects have more of a rental facility which goes into one facility, etc., there are a number of factors there. ASHWIN MEHTA: I just note that for the last three quarters it's been in the 140 crore range and it's fallen off to nearly 80 crores in this one. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Yes, it's -- as I said it's would -- increase beyond what is currently shown in this quarter and this is not a sustainable one. ASHWIN MEHTA: Okay. DEEPAN KAPADIA, ANALYST, PROACTIVE UNIVERSAL GROUP: Congratulations on the quarter. Geography for you is expanding in China. What role does China play in the long run? And how much difference would it make to our margins? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: We are very small in China. (multiple speakers) too, too small for it to today make any difference. I think it is going to be quite a long time before we can talk about material impact on a quarterly revenue basis or margin basis. At this point in time all I would say, that we are keen that we go there faster, much, much faster than what we are doing now. And we have a long way to go. So I don't think that

we should worry about it now. DEEPAN KAPADIA: Okay. One more point is, do we feel that utilization and margins have peaked, or do we see some more betterment coming in from the next quarters? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: On the utilization, as I said, as long as there is a very strong demand, the pressure on utilization will always be there. We have gone up to 83.8% utilization in spite of addition of 19%, 293 people gross. So that basically is because, as I said, when there is a strong demand on rental utilization will always will go up. And I think there is not an effort to push it to 85% or 86% or anything like that. I think we are recruiting, trying to be efficient, but we are not trying to push it. On the margin front, I think I have made all the comments and we are not able to comment at this point. DEEPAN KAPADIA: Thanks a lot. OPERATOR: Thank you. Ladies and gentlemen, due to time constraints, we will take one last question from the line of [Pranav Deluca] from Brics Securities. Please go ahead. PRANAV DELUCA, ANALYST, BRICS SECURITIES: Hello? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Hello. PRANAV DELUCA: Hello. Sir, I wanted to ask about the SG&A details, SG&A cost. The bad debt provisions have been decreasing from Q1 FY11 and Q2 FY11, from [245]. Any reasons for that? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: No. This follows a very clear policy in terms of collecting and so on. So there is no pattern of -- there is nothing unusual about it. PRANAV DELUCA: Okay. So will this be reversing in the coming quarters? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Essentially, the question is whether we have over provided earlier in order to take it out now. That is not. So I don't think -- of course we have been -- if you see the pattern. We have been providing and so on, and we may call these attempts to collect it a year after we make the provision because it's one of the certain principles. PRANAV DELUCA: Okay, sir. Thank you. OPERATOR: Thank you. I

would now like to hand the floor back to Mr. Kedar Shirali and the management for closing comments. Please go ahead, sir. KEDAR SHIRALI: Thank you, and in summary I would like to thank all of you for the confidence in TCS, and also for listening to us today in this call. And the results have been very, very good from our point of view. And we see the demand environment picking up quite strong. And we are getting all of our engines aligned to capture the demand and to respond to the market situation. Those are the final comments I'd like to say, and thank you very much. OPERATOR: Thank you, gentleman of the management. Thank you, Mr. Shirali. Ladies and gentlemen, on behalf of TCS, that concludes this conference call. Thank you for joining us on the Chorus Call conferencing service, and you may now disconnect your lines.
