With regard to the operating margin assumption decline of 100 basis points for the fiscal year, I was wondering how we should contemplate the impact of Nipuna. I know that you've described it as breakeven, but do you have any updated thoughts with regard to the trends at Nipuna? V. SRINIVAS: This is Srinivas here. We are not basically giving guidance subsidiary wise. What technically -- what basically I'm saying is that the impact of subsidiaries on our margins is going to be very, very minimal. When compared to last year - that is FY '06, FY '07 - the impact of subsidiaries is going to be very minimal. And the

operating level impact will have a positive impact. JAMES FRIEDMAN: Okay. And then, with regard to the U.S. GAAP, Srinivas, on the other income of [15.7m], that was much higher than when we were internally modeling. Could you help us decompose why that was so high in other income? V. SRINIVAS: That includes around $9.8m of ForEx gain. If we exclude that, then the balance reflects the interest income. JAMES FRIEDMAN: Okay. And then, finally, maybe this question is best for Ram. But we were wondering about the duration of some of the contracts. It seems like you had done very well in the consulting business in the fiscal Q1. It was up 10% sequentially. How should we think about the duration of that? Is that short term in nature? Will it need to be replaced in the back half of the year? Or does it inform the application development maintenance work that you also do? RAM MYNAMPATI: Well, first of all, any [inaudible] including consulting tends to have a lifecycle of its own. While a consulting engagement comes to an end, it gets replaced by other consulting opportunities. But more important is that we are hopeful and the reality is very much in line with that, is that every consulting engagement leads to downstream revenue opportunities of different types. Sometimes it is application maintenance; sometimes it's ERP implementation, whatever. So, to us, we cannot really predict the sustainability of any revenue stream across a quarter or two. What we need to do is look at whether a given line of business is contributing to the overall growth of the organization. Sometimes, as I said, it's downstream revenue, sometimes it is cross-sell opportunities. Sometimes, it is building upon what we are able to deliver as value. So it's very difficult to predict any kind of sustainability to any revenue stream. As I said, we would not be in a position to do that. JAMES FRIEDMAN: Okay, thank you very much. OPERATOR: Your next question comes from the line of Alan Hellawell with Lehman Brothers. ALAN HELLAWELL, ANALYST, LEHMAN BROTHERS: Yes,

congratulations, gentlemen. Just one maybe broad question. There seems to be further encouraging evidence of penetration of larger accounts. Can you give us a little more color behind that? What key organizational initiatives have you taken and maybe -- or to state it another way, what are you offering a large account this year that same time last year you would not have been able to offer? B. RAMA RAJU: Quite some time ago, we have created what we refer to as a strategic deals group to particularly focus on large deals. And that group has operated in a very focused fashion. And we believe that that has certainly helped. We have not only closed a few sizeable deals but we have a fair amount in the pipeline. ALAN HELLAWELL: Okay, I'm sorry. Is that the end of your response? RAM MYNAMPATI: Yes. Well, let me just add to what Raju said. Not only have we focused on pursuing opportunities that are larger in size and longer in duration, if you look at the value proposition that ultimately we offer to the market today, there are two types. One is a value proposition that relates to total accountability and ownership to delivering a broader set of services. So to that end, we are playing the role of -- we are continuing to play the role of a systems integrator, where a customer is looking to a partner to take accountability to deliver business value according to a specific set of SLAs or whatever. Second is that, when we talk about large deals, we're not only talking about a specific service offering. We are talking about integrated solutions. To that end, we would be in a unique position to put together, from among all the service offerings that are available within the organization as well as outside, the most integrated value that we can put together that would benefit the customer. To that end, large deals tend to be a go-to-market strategy for more than one service offering. Sometimes it is infrastructure management; sometimes it is engineering services; sometimes it is

enterprise applications; sometimes it is BPO. So what it would offer to us is a fairly unique opportunity of integrating all the services that we have brought to a certain level of maturity and relate to the right business value that we are able to deliver to the customer. So what we offer today, number one is a capability to integrate all the services into specific business solutions. Number two is the capability to take end-to-end responsibility and accountability to deliver to the set of SLAs. Those are the things that we are focusing on maturing, in addition to having a dedicated focus and a go-to-market strategy to pursue opportunities of that kind. ALAN HELLAWELL: I appreciate that. Could I just ask you? Being as general as you want, can you characterize the services that you've brought into play in one of the more recent large contracts, just to get a flavor of what horizontals you're bundling together, etc? Can you give me an example? RAM MYNAMPATI: Sure. Without naming a customer specifically, I would say the three different service offerings that we have been able to put together included ADMS, enterprise applications and IMS, infrastructure management. So we are able to not only provide services on the infrastructure management side, enterprise application maintenance side, as well as ancillary integrate applications. But, more importantly, we are able to work with the client in identifying a roadmap of what solutions that we can deliver in an integrated fashion, while taking individual accountability for each of those services. ALAN HELLAWELL: Okay. And my last, very short question, oftentimes when you move into a new market that is dominated by incumbents, the value proposition is the first step you take. You offer a better value per dollar, and then you start building around it with follow-on services. What do you feel or what do you know to be the kind of discount on services that you're able to offer at this early stage relative to the other guys? RAM MYNAMPATI: Well, again, it's very difficult to, number one, generalize, and number two, quantify

those kinds of things. Ultimately it is no longer measured in a tactical, cost per service basis. I think the customers today are looking increasingly to the total cost take-out, and that tends to be spread over a much longer period, and it tends to be much more integrated and complex than the tactical cost per dollar that we think goes into the service. Even if we offer the same service for a few dollars less, to the extent that the total cost of managing that service is better for the customer, it is unlikely that we would be able to convince the customer of the value prop. So the value proposition consists of a number of things - competency, price, the ability to deliver and, more importantly, the time period within which we are able to convince the customer. I don't know that we can be any more specific than that, other than to say it is customer specific and situation specific as far as value prop is concerned. ALAN HELLAWELL: That very much paints the picture. Thank you very much. RAM MYNAMPATI: Thank you. OPERATOR: Your next question comes from the line of Anthony Miller with Arete Research. ANTHONY MILLER, ANALYST, ARETE RESEARCH: Yes, thanks, gentlemen. I need some clarification, please, both on the margin issue and also on campus recruitment. On margins, I'm struggling here, particularly looking at your gross margin. If you look at in U.S. GAAP it seems that you've dropped about 300 basis points of gross margin, sequentially. Even if you put back the share-based payment, that comes to about 200 basis points light, and that's in a very positive environment. You've already talked about the pricing was up, the utilization is about steady, offshore is up. All the main leaders are going in the right direction. Now, I take on board that you've brought on more people in the fourth quarter, but there again, so have all the other players. When I look at, for example, Wipro, who like you have not yet given your salary -- given salary hikes, their gross margin

was up 300 basis points in the first quarter. And even if I look at you this time last year, first quarter '06, your gross margin was up about 140 basis points. I really can't understand why there was such a hit on your gross margin in the first quarter. Can you help me on that, please? V. SRINIVAS: Yes. This is Srinivas here clarifying. This includes the stock-based charge under FAS123R of 3.7m. This was not there in the last quarter. ANTHONY MILLER: No, but even accounting for that, that still brings the margin drop to about 200 basis points instead of 300. It's not that significant. V. SRINIVAS: Yes. Then why I am talking about $6m of provisions we made. ANTHONY MILLER: Okay. V. SRINIVAS: In the compensation, towards the leave encashment that is an additional provision that came this time because of the accounting standard change in India. So that is another $6m. So if you take these two things into account, they explain predominantly the change. But I was also mentioning about the 3,000 people who we have recruited in Q4. Most of them joined in March. Their salary -- full salary impact is felt in Q1, whereas in Q4 when we're making quarter-on-quarter comparisons, the salary that we would have given in Q4 will be much lower. So these are basically the reasons for the cash flow revenue going up. ANTHONY MILLER: Okay. I'll take that offline with you, because even adding in those two items, I get basically flat gross margins, whereas what I would have expected was gross margin uplift because of all the other factors. But I won't take time on the call for that. My second question was just a bit on clarity on campus recruitment. Can you give us an idea, please, on this current recruitment cycle, how many universities -- how many universities and colleges you've already attended, how many more you have to go, how many offers you've made, what's been the acceptance rate, and what is your anticipated fresher mix this year, please? I know it sounds

like a long list. V. SRINIVAS: We have not been giving such a detailed information as that. It goes without saying that we are trying to access talent from many institutions for [inaudible], and this is looking across the [inaudible]. Suffice it to say that we are tapping the opportunity to get the best talent in. ANTHONY MILLER: But can I -- sorry to interrupt. Can I at least ask, then, do you feel compared to this time last year that you are being more successful in recruiting freshers, or is it the same, or less successful, both in terms of finding the right people and them accepting? B. RAMA RAJU: We have never set any challenge about being able to recruit people at the entry level as such, and we continue to get the right kind of people that meet our requirements. And in that sense, the challenges have been greater when it comes to lesser recruitment than entry level recruitment. And of late, we have -- for example last quarter, the proportion of our entry level people has been higher, and we expect that this trend matched at one or two quarters the entry level of recruitment to be at a higher level than at lesser levels. But in terms of attracting quality people, in spite of the environment being very competitive, we have not had challenges that we could not effectively address. ANTHONY MILLER: Okay. All right, then, thanks very much indeed. V. SRINIVAS: Anthony, before you sign off, one more important last element that has gone in this quarter is the [inaudible] expenditure. I think quarter-on-quarter this has been increasing around [5.5m] or so, [4.5m, 4 to 5.5m]. At the last quarter it was 1m. So quarter-on-quarter, there was an increase of roughly around $400m. ANTHONY MILLER: Yes, I'm sorry, I didn't hear what -- what was that cost alluding -- referring to? V. SRINIVAS: These are visa expenditures. Visa -- for applying to visas here, [inaudible] visas. ANTHONY MILLER: Oh, sorry. V. SRINIVAS: For the entire yearly requirement, we have applied now in this quarter

so we will take the hit in this quarter. There will be more in the rest of investment, because these visas we will be using up over the next 12 to 18 months. ANTHONY MILLER: Yes, indeed. In fact, one of your competitors has done exactly the same. That's great. I'll catch up with you afterwards offline, if I may. V. SRINIVAS: Sure, sure, most welcome. ANTHONY MILLER: Thanks very much. OPERATOR: Your next question comes from the line of Ashish Thadani with Gilford Securities. ASHISH THADANI, ANALYST, GILFORD SECURITIES INC: Yes, good evening, very nice quarter. I just wanted to focus on the ForEx issue. You had a gain of 9.8m net below the operating line in this quarter. It seems that you're getting help when you don't really need it from ForEx. The objective of a hedging program, I guess, is to generate a gain when the rupee is strong and vice versa, but that does not appear to be the case. How should we think about this issue, especially in an environment of rupee strength? Is it going to come back and hurt you? V. SRINIVAS: Yes. Ashish, Srinivas here. Yes, this quarter [inaudible] from the cash statement, we have been given this gain of around 14 -- $13.7m because of ForEx transactions, and other income includes 4.9m [inaudible] because of the hedging losses. So net/net it was $11.8m because of the ForEx. Yes, it is very difficult for us to completely seal ourselves from the vagaries of the ForEx movement, but [inaudible] a balanced strategy wherein we will be equally equipped whether rupee appreciates or depreciates. What I'm saying here is we have taken a policy to cover 50% of our dollar inflows in the next 12 months, and we are taking hedging steps. So to an extent the risk [inaudible] is the rupee. When the rupee depreciates we will not be gaining much. At the same time, when the rupee appreciates, also we will not be hurt that much. So that break is evenly spread. So to an extent, I think we're doing our best

under [inaudible]. ASHISH THADANI: Okay, and the 60 basis points impact in this quarter was above the line, right? V. SRINIVAS: Yes. ASHISH THADANI: Okay. And then just one other question. Your revenue performance in the quarter was ahead of guidance which you provided three months ago. And it appears that this may have come from -- possibly from new clients, which was above normal in terms of contribution to total revenue. Was this indeed the case? Was it across one or two clients or was it more broad-based? RAM MYNAMPATI: Ashish, this is Ram. It's certainly broad-based. But clearly the contribution is better from new clients, as you can see. The contribution from new clients is up this quarter, but it's not yet [inaudible] broad-based. ASHISH THADANI: Thank you very much. Good luck. RAM MYNAMPATI: Thank you. OPERATOR: Your next question comes from the line of Bhuvnesh Singh with Credit Suisse. BHUVNESH SINGH, ANALYST, CREDIT SUISSE FIRST BOSTON: Hi, sir, I just wanted to understand your margin guidance. Like you said, that the key reason why you're not increasing your margin guidance is because you're not sure of rupee/dollar. But what if that rupee/dollar stays at the level that it is? Then in that case will you have higher margins or -- what I'm trying to say, is there a degree of conservatism built in your guidance, or why has margin guidance not changed probably? V. SRINIVAS: Well, Bhuvnesh, it's what I was mentioning, that should rupee depreciate further, below the level predicted in the guidance, I think we will be [listening] and plowing it back into the divisions. That is one limited opportunity we have. BHUVNESH SINGH: Okay. You are doing the same thing in your revenue guidance, if I look at the revenue numbers. Probably all the increase in guidance can be explained by the shift between rupee/dollar and rupee/pound or euro. So it does not even take into account extra volume growth that you have had in this quarter. Is there some reason behind that or -- I'm not able to figure out that, why is

that the case? B. RAMA RAJU: No, I think -- I think [inaudible]. V. SRINIVAS: No, this is whatever improvement in performance is not getting reflected is in on the guidance for the year at the bottom-line level. B. RAMA RAJU: Is that your question, Bhuvnesh? BHUVNESH SINGH: What I am trying to say is that if I take your full guidance and the new guidance on top line, on revenues, all the change can be explained just by the shift in currency, rupee/dollars and rupee/euro. So while this quarter we have had something like 4% extra volume growth compared to what we guided initially. So why haven't we changed our full year guidance by more than the impact of currency? V. SRINIVAS: No, no, no, no. That is not correct. There is definitely rupee impact. If you're talking about the bottom line, I think the question answer is to look at the U.S. GAAP guidance. There are items that reflect the current situation wherein we increased our [bottom] from 26 to 27.3. So that 1.3 is entirely because of the dollar growth versus rupee, what [inaudible] you're saying, out of that roughly 1.3% is because of the business or the up-tick we are seeing in Q1 that's already in the bag. And the balance is because of the rupee. So it is a combination. It is not that the entire increase is because of the rupee. BHUVNESH SINGH: So, on that, [have you built the] change in rupee/euro aspect, because the euro has appreciated much more strongly than dollars? V. SRINIVAS: Yes, it has appreciated more than 9% while dollars appreciated by around 3.4%. Well, what it was [inaudible] it's an average of all the currencies. BHUVNESH SINGH: Okay, so I'll maybe take it offline to understand that better. Thanks a lot. V. SRINIVAS: Yes. OPERATOR: At this moment, there are no further questions from participants here. I would like to hand over the proceedings back to Pratibha. OPERATOR: Thank you very much, April. At this moment, I would like to hand over the floor back to the Satyam

management for final remarks. V. SRINIVAS: Well, thank you, Pratibha. I would like to take this opportunity to thank every one of you for joining us and actually participating on the call. If you have any further questions, please do contact us. And if you want further information on the results for this quarter, they have been posted on our website, satyam.com. Please do visit us. You have an Investors link, and also all of the accounts are already included there. Please do look into it. And if you have any questions, please get in touch with our Investor Relations team. Thank you once again. OPERATOR: Ladies and gentlemen, thank you for choosing Webex Conferencing Service. That concludes this conference call. Thank you for your participation. You may now disconnect your lines. Thank you and goodnight. Q4 2006 Satyam Computer Services Limited Earnings Conference Call - Final https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/q4-2006-satyam-computer-services-limited-earnings/docview/466282959/se-2?accountid=27562 Abstract: None available. Full text: OPERATOR: Good evening, ladies and gentlemen. I'm Prathibha, the moderator for this conference. Welcome to the Satyam conference call. For the duration of the presentation, all participant lines will be in the listen-only mode. The question and answer session will be conducted first for participants connected to India bridge, followed by the question and answer session for participants at the international bridge. I would now like to hand over to Mr. Srinivas Vadlamani. Thank you and over to you, sir. SRINIVAS VADLAMANI, CFO, SATYAM COMPUTER SERVICES: Thank you, Prathibha. Good morning and good evening to you all and thank you for joining us to discuss our fourth quarter and fiscal '07 results. Joining me on this call are Raju, Ram and Venkatesh from Nipuna. And this time we also have Hari T., who heads our Global HR, Human Resources function. Before we start the discussion, I would like to draw your attention to the fact that during this call we may make certain forward-looking statements concerning our future growth prospects. Such statements involve a number of risks and uncertainties associated with our business. Please refer to our various periodic filings with the SEC for a description of such risks. The Company does not undertake to update any forward-looking statement that may be made from time to time, by or on behalf of the Company. I now hand over the session to Raju. B. RAMALINGA RAJU, FOUNDER & CHAIRMAN, SATYAM COMPUTER SERVICES: Thank you, Srinivas. Good morning and good evening. Thank you for joining us on the call today. I am pleased to report that the Company has achieved an annual revenue growth of 35% and net profit growth of 43% under Indian GAAP consolidated basis for fiscal 2007. As per U.S. GAAP, the revenue growth rate is 33% and the net income growth is 40%. The strong performance is a culmination of strategic initiatives taken by the Company on several fronts, notably in the areas of relationship management, deepening of competencies and associate

delight. We added 138 customers in fiscal 2007, including seven Fortune 500 customers. The number of customers billing more than US$10m increased to 35 from 27 during the year, while the number of customers billing more than US$1m increased by 20% to 180. The revenue from consulting and enterprise business solutions grew 43% in the year, indicating our dominant position in this segment. We continue to win the confidence of our prominent customers, resulting in opportunities of significant size and criticality. Our recent win of a US$200m deal from Applied Materials in Q4 based on managed services model is illustrative of this trend. This has been complemented by our successful execution capabilities resulting in higher growth of such accounts. We continue to service our customers in areas of strategic importance such as delivering greater proportion of work from offshore. Significant progress was made in this area in fiscal 2007, leading to an increase in the offshore contribution to 49% of revenues compared to 45% in fiscal 2006. This was driven by a double-digit offshore volume growth in each of the quarters. The net addition of associates during the year is around 11,000, taking the total organization strength to around 40,000 at the close of this year. A noteworthy feature of the year has been the reduction in associate attrition to 15.7% from 19.2% in fiscal 2006. Annualized quarterly attrition for Q4 was 13%. We are gratified by the fact that our People Practices have won several recognitions in the global and regional arena in the past few months, which is an attestation of our renewed focus and commitment in this area. The latest feather in the cap is being accorded the number two position in the Hewitt Best Employers in India survey. We also featured amongst the top 12 in the Hewitt Best Employers in Asia survey, the only IT services company from India in the top 20 rankings. Q1 of fiscal 2008 will be witness to Satyam's completion of 20 years, an important milestone in the organization's journey of value creation. It symbolizes the maturity and strength of our

organization. We continue to see a strong demand in the marketplace and are well positioned to address that demand. Against this backdrop, we look forward to a revenue growth of 28% to 30% in fiscal 2008 as per U.S. GAAP. We expect to record a revenue of US$1.9b in fiscal 2008, at the higher end of our revenue guidance. The EPADS is expected to grow in the range of 27% to 29%. Corresponding Indian GAAP consolidated guidance after factoring in a rupee appreciation of 6% would translate to revenue growth rate of 20% to 22% and an EPS growth of 18% to 20%. The Board has proposed a final dividend of 125%. The total dividend for the fiscal 2007 stands at 175%, including the interim dividend of 50%. I take this opportunity to inform that Mr. V. P. Rama Rao, Director of our Board, has decided to step down from the Board of Directors due to personal reasons. I would like to place on record our appreciation for his contribution towards the growth of Satyam. I am pleased to inform you that Mr. T. R. Prasad, ex-Cabinet Secretary, Government of India and Member of the 12th Finance Commission and Dr. V. S. Raju, presently Chairman of Naval Research Board, Defense Research and Development Organization, Government of India and Director, Bharti Airtel Limited, will join Satyam's Board as Additional Directors with immediate effect. I am sure that the new members will contribute significantly to the Company's management, governance and technology leadership. I now request Srinivas to discuss the financial highlights for [Q3]. SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: Thank you, Raju. Our detailed financials have been placed on our website and I hope most of you would have had an opportunity to go through the same. However, I will focus on some of the important highlights of Q4 as well as the fiscal '07. In Q4 our revenues grew 9.5% sequentially in U.S. dollar terms and over 7% in rupee terms, as rupee appreciated 1.5% during the quarter. Net profit for the quarter grew 17% sequentially on the back of the higher revenue growth. Sequential

volume growth of the parent company was 9.5%, with offshore volumes growing at 12%. Offshore billing rates were up by 0.7% and onsite rates increased by 0.6%. Based on the contract renegotiations that we had in the last year and the rates at which new customers are coming in, we are confident that the positive momentum seen in the billing rates in Q4 will continue in FY '08 also. Operating margins under consolidated Indian GAAP were at 23.1% after factoring in a stock-based compensation charge of INR16 crores and 1.5% rupee appreciation. For fiscal '07, operating margins declined by 60 basis points compared to fiscal '06, in line with our earlier guidance. EBITDA margins for fiscal '06 are expected to be at the same level as fiscal '07 after considering salary increments, over 6% rupee appreciation and absorbing a charge of US$20m on account of RSUs given in fiscal '07. Net manpower addition was 165 [sic - see documentation] in Q4 for the parent company and includes 600 ELTPs, that is freshers. We expect the gross manpower addition for fiscal '08 to be around 14,000 to 15,000. Nipuna, our BPO subsidiary, had a sequential growth of 18% in revenues. For Q4 Nipuna reported revenues of US$11.5m and a loss of US$0.2m. For fiscal '07, Nipuna recorded revenues of US$38m, a growth of 91% over fiscal '06, and was EBITDA positive for the entire year. For fiscal '08, Nipuna is expected to achieve revenue of US$61m, a 60% year-on-year growth, and is expected to do positive at the net profit level. Turning to the U.S. GAAP, revenue for the quarter rose 9.5% sequentially to US$411.3m. And net income for the quarter was US$86.3m, a sequential growth of 21% after factoring in a stock compensation charge of US$5.9m. As Raju mentioned, under U.S. GAAP revenue for fiscal '08 is expected to grow by 28% to 30% and net income is expected to grow by 30% to 32% after factoring in a stock compensation charge of US$28m. For fiscal '07, we have flat operating margin compared to fiscal '06 and we expect that

trend to continue in fiscal '08 also. Earning per ADS is expected to grow between 27% to 29%, marginally lower than the net income growth because of 2% plus dilution in the share count on account of exercise of options by our associates. Now I'll stop here. Thank you. We will throw open the session for Q&A. OPERATOR: Thank you very much, sir. We will now begin the Q&A interactive session for participants connected to Webex India. [OPERATOR INSTRUCTIONS]. Participants are kindly requested to restrict to two questions in the initial round. Please use only handsets while asking your question. [OPERATOR INSTRUCTIONS]. The first question comes from the line of Mr. Sameer Goyal with Alchemy Shares. SAMEER GOYAL, ANALYST, ALCHEMY SHARES: Yes, hi. Good evening. Great performance. My question pertains to shape of offshore; that's happening at a very rapid pace. So could you elaborate a bit more the reasons for the same? And where do you feel comfortable offshore being at? RAM MYNAMPATI, MEMBER OF THE BOARD & PRESIDENT, COMMERCIAL & HEALTHCARE BUSINESSES, SATYAM COMPUTER SERVICES: Yes. Sameer, this is Ram Mynampati here. You know the percentage growth in offshore is driven -- one second please, we're hearing some echo here. Prathibha, is there a problem with the line? OPERATOR: Mr. Goyal? SAMEER GOYAL: Yes. OPERATOR: Are you able to hear the speaker clearly, sir? SAMEER GOYAL: Yes. RAM MYNAMPATI: Okay, I will continue. The increase in contribution from offshore is resulting from two things. One is a conscious effort from our side to move as much work offshore as possible. And that is also supported by the maturity of the relationships that we have today. Number two is that, given the delivery models that we continue to engage in, many of the new technologies and new delivery models lend themselves well to distributed delivery and that's also contributing to the increased efforts from offshore. on the type of services that we deliver, the maturity of the customers and our ability to identify the right processes to make the service delivery appropriate from offshore. I wanted to find out from Hari what do you think is a gap at this point in time between you and some of your other Tier 1 competitors on the onsite wage increase? Factoring in what you guys expect to do going forward and what you did last year, how far off are you from some of your largest competitors at this point in time? HARI T.: Sure. Again, this is Hari. To kind of give a small backdrop to it, every year we do the compensations which takes into account some of the large Indian players as well as taking into account some of the global systems integrators who operate in different countries. So I would say that last year, by virtue of enhanced rates, we were able to

bring out and bridge some of the gaps that existed. And by virtue of what we are trying to do this year and what I have seen in that market, we believe that there will be no major gaps. In fact, I would say we are very much competitive with all the large Indian IT players overseas. JULIO QUINTEROS: Okay. Great. And then for Mynampati on the -- excuse me, on the question about margins, as you expect to ramp-up some of these large contracts, how do you think about the margin profile of these larger contracts and how they impact the quarterly progression? So if you can kind of walk us through what you think about that or -- and then sort of as you think about the longer term with some of these other big contracts coming on line, especially relative to your guidance, are they necessary in order to for you make the 28 to 30% revenue guidance? Sorry, I meant for Srinivas. SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: On a typical large deal, we are taking an approach that we will not compromise on margins. We believe that our deals also most often offer many creative opportunities to enhance margins while continuing to deliver high value to the customers. And there may be incentives which are somewhat raised, which may pertain to that category which is not to be defined as typical but we are making sure that we are not compromising on margins in general. JULIO QUINTEROS: Okay. And then relative to the guidance, the question I asked about how many large deals you needed to make your numbers for FY '08? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: No. I was merely illustrating that while large deals are important for us and new business development is important, the fact that we work for 165 Fortune 500 companies, and the fact that are growing even at 25th or 30th customer 40% or 50% potentially, each one of those would be equal in large deals of the size of $200m to $300m. Therefore, we are not to, in our opinion, read too much into large

deals; they are great. But the fact that we have a very satisfied client base is of even greater importantance. JULIO QUINTEROS: Okay. Great. Thank you. OPERATOR: Your next question comes from the line of George Price of Stifel Nicolaus. GEORGE PRICE, ANALYST, STIFEL NICOLAUS: Hi. Thanks very much. I just wanted to go back and ask you a couple of questions around your -- the margin headwinds. I guess you laid out what the headwinds are and what the levers are but can you a little bit more specifically give us some sense of what kind of offsetting benefit you expect from each of those levers? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: George, this is Srinivas here. I may not be able to quantify while we have in terms of [every case] but we're not able to counter each individual lever. But the most important levers we have is really the pricing. We've been talking about 2 to 3% increase on an average basis, which should have an impact of roughly around 200 basis points at the EBIT level. And offshore contribution, yes, it all depends on how much we can pick it up but this is 1% at the moment. But offshore will increase our margins by roughly around 20 to 25 basis points but that is one metric we have. Then, of course, subsidiary performance, it will not be as high as last year. For example, FY '07 our subsidiary performance added roughly around 70 basis points at the EBIT level. But next year it will not be as high as that but definitely it will be sizable. Then, of course, we have other levers like SG&A and, again, offshore/onsite rotation and various other levers which are available to us. Should -- I think at this point I'm feeling quite comfortable. GEORGE PRICE: Okay. And just specifically to understand pricing, is the 2 to 3%, is that on average what you're seeing in terms of the improvement of new deals and then renegotiated deals as they start up? And will it take time for that to roll through your portfolio business,

you're not going to get all of them to go all at once and capture that full pricing benefit? Or is the actual pricing improvement that you're realizing higher and the 2 to 3% is sort of what you expect to capture over the next year? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: When we refer to 2 to 3%, we are talking about weighted average or our growth in the client base. When it comes to new deals, our existing contracts being renegotiated, the proportion of the increase on an average was six months later. Obviously, new deals will start impacting the price progressively. And, therefore, it is what we do with the existing clients, both at the level of renegotiating contracts but, more importantly, how we manage the projects and realize operational efficiencies by addressing productivity issues and management issues. So those are the things that would revert in the average price being at what we have referred to as 2 to 3%. It's an aggregated figure. GEORGE PRICE: Okay. Right. No, understood. Thank you for the clarification. One last thing, the gross adds of 14,000 to 15,000, can you split that up between BPO and IT? HARI T.: This is Hari here. The 14,000 to 15,000 that we talked about was for Satyam Computer Services, the parent company. But I have Venkatesh here and he will be able to talk a bit about BPO. VENKATESH RODDAM, CEO, NIPUNA: This is Venkatesh here. Just to give you a quick update, we've closed the year at about close to 3,000 people and one important trend we're beginning to see is the dealing from number of people to revenue. So one of the clear trends we have established in the numbers at this time is to clearly mention that -- is to clearly see a trend which shows a dealing for the correlation between revenue growth versus headcount numbers. On a broad basis, we see this moving towards between 3,000 and 5,000 as the number during the course of this year. GEORGE PRICE: Great. Thank you. OPERATOR: Your next question comes from the line of

Joseph Foresi of Janney Montgomery Scott. JOSEPH FORESI, ANALYST, JANNEY MONTGOMERY SCOTT: Hi, guys. A couple of quick questions here. First, on the hiring front, if we look at those 14,000 to 15,000, that's ahead of last year's 11,000. It seems to coincide with your revenue, your higher revenue growth projections. Can we look at that as sort of an initial high-end guidance here? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: Last year 11,000 was the net increase, whereas what we are guiding for is a gross increase of 14,000 to 15,000. JOSEPH FORESI: So, just roughly speaking, so if we take about 10% out, we're still a little bit ahead of the net increases that you finished last year with, right? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: We do not know and we will have to see as to how the system will behave. But, generally speaking, it is reasonable to assume that it is, as Roddam said, what it has been last year. JOSEPH FORESI: Okay. And so I guess, let me put it to you this way, do you expect to hire more net adds this year or do you expect it to be the same as last year? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: We are not giving guidance at the next level at this time. JOSEPH FORESI: Okay. And my second question here is on the demand environment. We've heard a lot of talk about North America and financial services but these quarter's numbers seem to imply that package implementation is still pretty strong. Are you seeing a differentiation in the buying habits of what happened in financial services and package implementation? Is it safe to say that package competition is still going strong and application development may have just sort of paused here? RAM MYNAMPATI: This is Ram Mynampati here. I was answering this question in a different way a few minutes ago. There are clearly two trends that we are seeing. One is to see better package implementation as a business line. Is it expanding the initial breadth of our capability? We are seeing packages that are addressing a broader section of the business needs today

than has ever been the case before. So there is an attractive alternative to custom development available in packages and that is driving part of the package growth. The second is the opportunity for outsourcing to a large extent non-critical business functions through established packages. That is also driving some of the growth on packages. So I'm not sure whether I would relate the growth in package implementation to financial services in any way in terms of a trend of slowdown or whatever. But what we are seeing is a continuous strong demand in not only package implementations but the surrounding areas that include subsequent maintenance, subsequent opportunities related to BPO and the entire gamut of services. So it's fair to say that we are seeing a robust demand for our package implementation. JOSEPH FORESI: Okay. And just two more quick ones, if I could. On the large deal signings, can we expect there to be some lumpiness on top line growth now that you're seeing the ramp-up and ramp-down of different deals? Would one particular ramp-up on a large contract cause growth to accelerate quickly in a quarter? RAM MYNAMPATI: Well, actually, like any other distributed environment, there are obviously some sections that would grow faster than others. I would think they tend to even out during the course of a year. There might be some [difference] in one quarter due to one segment or the other but I would not necessarily look at that to be anything significant. JOSEPH FORESI: Okay. RAM MYNAMPATI: Given that our revenue base is today quite substantial, those variations are likely to exist. JOSEPH FORESI: My last quick question here is just on the other income level. Can you guys give us, just for modeling purposes, some idea of what you're expecting in other income broken down annually or sequentially? And thanks, guys. SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: We basically expect other income to be in the range of around $15m a quarter. JOSEPH FORESI: Okay. Thanks, guys. OPERATOR: Your next question comes from the line of Rod Bourgeois of Bernstein. ROD BOURGEOIS, ANALYST, SANFORD BERNSTEIN:

Hey, guys. I just wanted to enquire about the fixed price contracts. It sounds like you're looking at the movement towards fixed price contracts as something that could enhance your margins going forward. And, if that's the case, I wondered if you could help us understand, in your current portfolio deals, when you compare the margins on fixed price deals to the margins on time and materials based deals, what the margin differential is in those two different sets of contract types. SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: At this time it is reasonable to assume that they are not variant; they are somewhat similar. But this is where we expect to see significant opportunities for realizing a higher level of operational efficiencies. ROD BOURGEOIS: Okay. Do you have current fixed price contracts in your portfolio that are expected to have big swings in margins over the next year? In other words, is it possible that in your portfolio there's some deals that have been earning sub-par margins in the past that are currently on the upswing and that potentially being one of the factors that will help your margins in fiscal '08? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: There are [inaudible] of fixed bid projects at any given time. One has to expect that there will be a fair amount of variation. We, obviously, will not be in a position to share details of any given segment or for fixed bid projects. Suffice it to say that on an average we are managing the fixed [rates] such that they are not compromising the prices at which the contracts are priced or implied contract prices. We are, as I have stated, looking to bring greater efficiencies into those operations. Specific -- there may be a few instances where we may be gaining significantly. There may be instances where there are additional -- an additional effort that we are having to put in. ROD BOURGEOIS: Okay. I got it. And then, can you talk for a second about the fringe benefit tax, in terms of the effect that it will have on your employees as they exercise equity compensation,

and if you have a plan to address that? This was definitely an issue that came up about a month ago with the fringe benefit tax and the effect it would have on the equity compensation scheme. SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: At this time the government's situation is still somewhat unclear. We are waiting for some details to still emerge. I would -- yes, therefore, I think it would be premature to comment on the same. ROD BOURGEOIS: Okay. Well, I definitely agree that it's unclear. If the current language remains intact in terms of the fringe benefit tax, will you do something to help your employees mitigate the tax expense that will occur when they exercise their equity compensation? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: We are not wanting to take any position in the absence of clarity. ROD BOURGEOIS: Okay. Great. Hopefully we will get that clarity. I appreciate it. Thanks, guys. SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: Thank you. OPERATOR: Your next question comes from the line of Trip Chowdhry of Global Equities Research. TRIP CHOWDHRY, ANALYST, GLOBAL EQUITIES RESEARCH: Congratulations on a very good execution. Two questions, first regarding infrastructure services. Have you seen any business pick up because, say, some companies may be rolling out Vista? Are you still wait and see for this [Vista] rollout? RAM MYNAMPATI: Well, we see all kinds of opportunities. Vista opportunities are probably a little premature yet. We are, obviously, foreseeing some opportunities occurring but I think for that opportunity to scale it will take some time. TRIP CHOWDHRY: The second question I had is your wonderful win with applied materials and you talk about managed services. Is this managed services the way some companies are providing remote management or is it managed service in the sense like you have people who are managing then these services themselves? What is the dynamics there? B. RAMALINGA RAJU: Let me put it somewhat differently. What we are talking about managed services is us, as an organization, taking greater accountability and the responsibility to manage the entire type of services that we deliver to the customer without any regard to the type

of resources deployed, the experience profile, the location the services are delivered from, etc., etc. And our progress and our quality is measured purely by the adherence to established service level demands in the contract. These tend to typically be aggregated across multiple service levels and clearly organizations like ourselves are expected to take accountability and responsibility to deliver in the most effective fashion in a truly win/win model. That's what a managed service model is. TRIP CHOWDHRY: One last question I had was regarding some trends that we are picking up, especially in the area here. There are some companies which are starting to evolve which are providing both software service and BPO services together, like a confluence of software delivery similar to sales force dotcom kind of stuff but having a BPO unit next to it. Do you think that the marketplace what these companies are trying to enter has some significant potential? VENKATESH RODDAM: Hi. This is Venkatesh Roddam here. If I can answer your question briefly, it's no different from the trend you are seeing in the Indian companies as well. If you look at the marketplace today, most significant BPO companies are an extension of a logical successful IT parent. So, to that extent, it [consumes] what the market is demanding today in terms of looking for single service providers with the ability to provide both IT and BPO deals and, therefore, the increasing availability of integrated deals. TRIP CHOWDHRY: Excellent. Thank you and congratulations on very good execution. SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: Thank you. OPERATOR: Your next question comes from the line of James Friedman of SIG. Mr. Friedman, your line is open. SAM SAUNDERS, ANALYST, SUSQUEHANNA INTERNATIONAL GROUP: Hi. This is Sam Saunders in for Jamie Friedman. Congratulations on a great quarter and good execution. I just had a question on some of the headcount trends. I noticed that the offshore utilization, including -- excuse me, excluding trainees is running a little hot. It's gone as high as 80%. I just wanted to understand how you plan to move more business offshore and maintain utilization

in a comfortable band. Thank you. HARI T.: We have been able to maintain healthy our inflation rate. And the fact that we are striving to enhance the offshore proportion is in no way contradicting our existing levels of utilization in offshore because that could as well come from the additional recruitment that we are doing. You have found that we have this year planned for as much as 14,000 to 15,000 people being added. Therefore, our attempt to enhance offshoring as a proportion would still be a feasible approach. SAM SAUNDERS: Great. Thank you. OPERATOR: Your next question comes from the line of Ashish Thadani of Gilford Securities. ASHISH THADANI, ANALYST, GILFORD SECURITIES: Yes. Good evening. Terrific quarter. I have a question for Srinivas. Under U.S. GAAP, you indicated that the stock compensation expense would be around $28m. Could you break that out by RSU and other items and also give a furnished number for '09 that's comparable to the $28m? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: Yes, Ashish. Srinivas here. It is $20m for RSUs and $8m for FAS 123. And the comparable numbers FY '09, I think FAS 123 will come down drastically and the RSUs will remain at around $14m, $15m. ASHISH THADANI: Okay. That's very helpful. Also, on the pricing, your comments are very encouraging. You indicated 2 to 3% for the coming year and that's built in. Are you assuming that onsite and offshore will increase in step or do you expect one to be a little higher than the other? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: Basically, I think it will be -- there are various scenarios, Ashish. Some time back, as I was explaining, this 2 to 3% average hike is what we have average. There are various scenarios here. It is not just that every customer gives 2 to 3%. There are various scenarios there. We are getting maybe 5 to 10% also and some customers they are not giving an increase at all. So net/net, similarly some customers they are never getting onsite increase from customers. Offshore, there are various combinations here and when we kind of work

it out net/net it is around 2 to 3%. ASHISH THADANI: Okay. Thank you very much and a great quarter. SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: Thank you. OPERATOR: Your next question comes from the line of Prashant Premkumar [inaudible]. PRASHANT PREMKUMAR, ANALYST: Okay. Good evening and thank you for taking my question. I just wanted to get a break-up, just going back to the margin question. Can you give me a break-up of the quarter-on-quarter decline of 160 bps at the EBITDA level? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: Sorry. I didn't get your question. 160 bps? PRASHANT PREMKUMAR: Can you give me the break-up of the EBITDA decline, margin decline at the EBITDA level? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: For Q4? PRASHANT PREMKUMAR: Yes, for Q4. That's right. SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: It is 162 basis points. PRASHANT PREMKUMAR: That's right? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: Yes. PRASHANT PREMKUMAR: Could you give us a break-up of that? B. RAMALINGA RAJU: Break-up, that was clarified -- I do not know if you have joined [late] in the call. We did clarify that. Maybe, Srinivas, you can repeat what you have stated. SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: Yes. You are saying which really contributed to this 162 basis point decline. That is what you are saying? PRASHANT PREMKUMAR: That's correct. SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: Yes. We have the -- to an extent [inaudible] that by around 125 basis points, another surcharge contributing to around 91 basis points. And [inaudible] is a saving of around 64 basis points. PRASHANT PREMKUMAR: Okay. SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: So that's the 162 basis point decline. PRASHANT PREMKUMAR: Okay. And on your guidance for about 5% onsite wage increase, what -- how comfortable are you with that, given what some of your competitors are doing and the levels at which your competitors are paying? HARI T.: Yes. This is Hari here. We look at the market landscape and we are noticing that they are talking about 15% offshore and about 3 to 5% onsite. Now, what we are talking about is 15% offshore and 5% onsite compares very well with that and we intend to strengthen our current position by going with this. So I would say that

it appears pretty comfortable. PRASHANT PREMKUMAR: Okay. Thanks. OPERATOR: We have a follow-up question from the line of Julio Quinteros of Goldman Sachs. JULIO QUINTEROS: Thank you. I actually wanted to go back to Hari real quickly. Can you go back and address the question on the entry level versus middle level, where you said that that's always where there's going to be more challenges in hiring employees? Can you give us a sense there on maybe what percentage of the expense structure does that middle level layer make up for you guys? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: Yes. This is Srinivas here. While we are basically stating the proportion of people that we are basically taking at the entry level, for level three and so on, in terms of specific numbers at each of the levels, we are not basically sharing in the public domain. So it suffices to say that here today there's going to be an impact of around 325 to 350 basis points in terms of the wage inflation. And we are basically taking steps to mitigate [in connection] if possible, maybe just some productivity enhancement and [other] measures. JULIO QUINTEROS: Okay. Got it. Thank you. OPERATOR: At this moment, there are no further questions from participants at WebEx International Center. I would like to hand over the proceedings back to Prathibha. OPERATOR: Thank you very much, Laurie. We will take a question from India next. Next question comes from the line of Mr. [Mayu] with [Wealth Managers]. MR. MAYU, ANALYST, WEALTH MANAGERS: I've got a question in terms of we are right now seeing computing and enterprise business solutions at around 41.5% of the total revenue and applications at around 47.5%. Do we see these both converging and around when? B. RAMALINGA RAJU: Well, it is very difficult to predict where these trends will move. What we are seeing is that there are going to be applications that would be subject, at some point, that are currently part of our maintenance portfolio. There will continue to be development efforts that are specific to custom applications that are not

addressed by enterprise applications. And, as enterprise applications become richer in their functionality, there will be basically a portion of the business [inaudible] where enterprise applications would be able to converge with one or take the other. These are all hypothetical questions that we won't have any answers to, other than to say that our continued strength in enterprise applications would put us in a stronger position to pursue opportunities in this space much better than others. Beyond that, it would be very difficult to predict any trends. MR. MAYU: Be focusing on the second aspect of the business? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: I'm sorry. MR. MAYU: You would be broadly focusing on the package implementation -- enterprise solutions? B. RAMALINGA RAJU: No. We have to focus on ADMS as well. It's not one at the cost of the other. All I'm saying is the enterprise applications tend to be of a different kind than the traditional ADMS applications. As we are pursuing newer opportunities, all we're saying is there is a likelihood of a greater percentage of newer opportunities being those enterprise applications. It doesn't mean that we don't focus on anything else. MR. MAYU: Okay. And the guidance that you have given or guidance now in terms -- or expectation that you are seeing of a rise of around 2 to 3% on a weighted average, in terms of service lines, where are you seeing this growth coming from, from service lines as well as from vertical lines? B. RAMALINGA RAJU: We are not giving guidance at such a fragmented level. I am sorry but we may not be able to share. MR. MAYU: But not numbers but [inaudible] which is higher, which is lower, but not give numbers? B. RAMALINGA RAJU: You mean at the technology level? MR. MAYU: Yes. At the technology level and also the line of business, both, please. B. RAMALINGA RAJU: Yes. As you are well aware, our enterprise business solutions has been a strong area for us. We have consistently grown and that will continue to be our strong area. Application development and maintenance

has traditionally been a very [interesting] service for us, so we are trying to package our solutions and make sure that we are providing high-quality solutions around the same. There are vertical markets emerging. We are quite pleased with the growth that we have seen in areas such as healthcare, retail, media and entertainment, energy and utilities. We had, again, a strong hold in areas such as automobile. We work for the majority of the [inaudible] [auto-makers] there. Therefore, that is a strong offering that we have. Geographically, we are quite pleased with the fact that the last quarter has witnessed a U.S. market contribution coming down below 50% and we have also [seen Europe] grow to 20%. We have -- last year, our performance in Australia has been extremely good. Australia, today, contributes [77%] of our revenues. So we have a much better distributed global delivery model today. Therefore, this is hopefully an indication of where we are seeing our strengths playing out. OPERATOR: Hello, sir. Mr. Mayu? MR. MAYU: Yes. Thank you very much. OPERATOR: Thank you very much, sir. Next question comes from the line of Mr. [Anurag Rana], [inaudible] Securities. ANURAG RANA, ANALYST: Good evening. My question is regarding the cost of delivery increase in the quarter. If you look at the standalone financial statement, you will see that the employee cost has increased much faster than the revenue growth. Any particular reason for this? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: This is Srinivas here. One of the reasons here is RSU charge. ANURAG RANA: Okay. How much was the RSU charge in the quarter, sir? SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: [INR16] crores. ANURAG RANA: Right. Thank you. Thank you very much. OPERATOR: Thank you very much, sir. Next is a follow-up question from Mr. Sandeep Shah, Motilal Oswal. SANDEEP SHAH: My questions have been answered. Thanks. OPERATOR: Thank you very much, sir. At this moment, there are no further questions from participants. I would like to hand over the floor back to Satyam management for final remarks. SRINIVAS VADLAMANI: Thank you, Prathibha. On behalf of the entire management team here, I would

like to thank everyone that's here for their active participation. As I was mentioning, our results are available on the website. Our investor link is there on the website with full details. If you have any further queries, please do contact us at Investor Relations at satyam.com. Thank you. OPERATOR: Ladies and gentlemen, thank you for choosing WebEx conferencing service. That concludes this conference call. Thank you for your participation. You may now disconnect your lines. Thank you and goodnight. Full text: OPERATOR: Good evening, ladies and gentlemen. I'm Pratibha, the moderator for this conference. Welcome to Satyam Investor Relations Conference Call. For the duration of the presentation, all participant lines will be in the listen-only mode. After the presentation, the question and answer session will be conducted. I would now like to hand over to the Satyam management. Thank you and over to Satyam. V. SRINIVAS, CFO, SATYAM: Thank you Pratibha. Good morning and good evening to you all and thank you for joining us to discuss our Q1 results. Joining me on this call are Raju and Ram from Satyam and Venkatesh from Nipuna. Before we start the discussion, I would like to draw your attention to the fact that during this call we may make certain forward-looking statements concerning our future growth prospects. Such statements involve a number of risks and uncertainties associated with our business. Please refer to our various periodic filings with SEC for the description of such risks. The Company does not undertake to update any forward-looking statements that may be made from time to time by or on behalf of the Company. I now hand over the session to Raju. B. RAMA RAJU, CEO, SATYAM: Thank you, Srinivas. Good evening and good morning to you and thank you for joining us on the call today. In Q1 the Company has reported a revenue of INR1,000 -- INR1443 crores, a sequential growth of 9.84% as per Indian GAAP consolidated financials. Earnings per share, at INR10.87, has grown by 23.67% sequentially. The revenue and earnings per share for Q1 have exceeded the forecasted range on the back of robust volume growth and favorable exchange rate movement during the quarter. The revenue growth has been fuelled by a 7.2% increase in volumes and higher contribution from offshore, both being critical initiatives that we focus on. The growth continues to be broad based across verticals and service offerings. The dispersion of growth across a large customer base is evident from

the higher growth recorded across the non 10 -- top 10 customers in Q1. We added 34 new customers in this quarter. Offshore contribution continued to increase in Q1, reflecting our maturity in leveraging the global delivery model effectively to deliver enhanced value for the customers. Over the last four quarters the offshore contribution has increased by 4% and now stands at 47%. Consulting and enterprise business services grew sequentially by 13% in Q1. It is heartening to note that relative to our peers we have the [world] position in the challengers' quadrant by Gartner in a report published on North American ERP service providers. We have made rapid progress in ramping up existing customers in the past few quarters. Our relationship management focus on leveraging opportunities across prominent customer accounts is amply reflected in the sequential increase of $10m customers from 27 to 33. The number of customers with an annualized billing exceeded $5m -- exceeding $5m has also increased sequentially from 46 to 51. This reiterates our customers' increasing confidence in our ability to deliver higher business value. This was also evidenced in the active support of over 250 [sic - see documentation] CXOs and senior business leaders and business partners who participated in our Customer Summit held in Q1. We are encouraged by the continued expansion of addressable market for global IT service providers. This is a result of expansion in service offerings and greater of global delivery model even by industries that have traditionally been conservative. This is a pointer towards a strong pipeline of opportunities across service offerings that we can deliver to the market. A complementary trend has been our enhanced participation in projects of larger size and longer duration. Our focus in enhancing the leadership bandwidth has become more pronounced in the recent past with the introduction -- with the induction of a number of senior leaders from reputed global organizations. The diversity of these additions is a reflection of the opportunities being addressed by us across various sectors and regions. We shall continue to make these investments in line with the anticipated

needs of our customers. Against this background, we are revising our annual guidance for fiscal 2007 upwards under Indian GAAP consolidated financials. Revenue growth is expected to be in the range of 29.2% to 31.2% and earnings per share is expected to grow between 27.5% to 29.5%. The continued positive sentiment prevalent in the industry is placing a premium on the availability of manpower with appropriate skill sets at all levels. Besides enhancing monetary benefits, we are also focused on creating an organizational environment which will help us attract and retain the best talent in the geographies that we operate in. One such initiative is the setting up of the Satyam School of Leadership to groom leaders as well as fulfill the learning needs of the associates. I am delighted to share with you that Satyam has been ranked as Number One -- Number Two global outsourcing vendor in a survey conducted by Brown-Wilson Group Inc. for the Black Book of Outsourcing's Top 50 Best Managed Companies, 2006. The same was driven by the sequential revenue growth of 9.8% and was further aided by the ForEx gain of US$9.8m. The Company saw 7.2% overall volume growth and average billing rate moved up marginally compared to previous quarter. Net manpower addition for the parent Company was 1123, including 457 freshers. this quarter so we will take the hit in this quarter. There will be more in the rest of investment, because these visas we will be using up over the next 12 to 18 months. ANTHONY MILLER: Yes, indeed. In fact, one of your competitors has done exactly the same. That's great. I'll catch up with you afterwards offline, if I may. V. SRINIVAS: Sure, sure, most welcome. ANTHONY MILLER: Thanks very much. OPERATOR: Your next question comes from the line of Ashish Thadani with Gilford Securities. ASHISH THADANI, ANALYST, GILFORD SECURITIES INC: Yes, good evening, very nice quarter. I just wanted to focus on the ForEx issue. You had a gain of 9.8m net below the operating line in this quarter. It seems that you're getting help when you don't really need it from ForEx. The objective of a hedging program, I guess, is to generate a gain when the rupee is strong and vice versa, but that does not appear to be the case. How should we think about this issue, especially in an environment of rupee strength? Is it going to come back and hurt you? V. SRINIVAS: Yes. Ashish, Srinivas here. Yes, this quarter [inaudible] from the cash statement, we have been given this gain of around 14 -- $13.7m because of ForEx transactions, and other income includes 4.9m [inaudible] because of the hedging losses. So net/net it was $11.8m because of the ForEx. Yes, it is very difficult for us to completely seal ourselves from the vagaries of the ForEx movement, but [inaudible] a balanced strategy wherein we will be equally equipped whether rupee appreciates or depreciates. What I'm saying here is we have taken a policy to cover 50% of our dollar inflows in the next 12 months, and we are taking hedging steps. So to an extent the risk [inaudible] is the rupee. When the rupee depreciates we will not be gaining much. At the same time, when the rupee appreciates, also we will not be hurt that much. So that break is evenly spread. So to an extent, I think we're doing

our best under [inaudible]. ASHISH THADANI: Okay, and the 60 basis points impact in this quarter was above the line, right? V. SRINIVAS: Yes. ASHISH THADANI: Okay. And then just one other question. Your revenue performance in the quarter was ahead of guidance which you provided three months ago. And it appears that this may have come from -- possibly from new clients, which was above normal in terms of contribution to total revenue. Was this indeed the case? Was it across one or two clients or was it more broad-based? RAM MYNAMPATI: Ashish, this is Ram. It's certainly broad-based. But clearly the contribution is better from new clients, as you can see. The contribution from new clients is up this quarter, but it's not yet [inaudible] broad-based. ASHISH THADANI: Thank you very much. Good luck. RAM MYNAMPATI: Thank you. OPERATOR: Your next question comes from the line of Bhuvnesh Singh with Credit Suisse. BHUVNESH SINGH, ANALYST, CREDIT SUISSE FIRST BOSTON: Hi, sir, I just wanted to understand your margin guidance. Like you said, that the key reason why you're not increasing your margin guidance is because you're not sure of rupee/dollar. But what if that rupee/dollar stays at the level that it is? Then in that case will you have higher margins or -- what I'm trying to say, is there a degree of conservatism built in your guidance, or why has margin guidance not changed probably? V. SRINIVAS: Well, Bhuvnesh, it's what I was mentioning, that should rupee depreciate further, below the level predicted in the guidance, I think we will be [listening] and plowing it back into the divisions. That is one limited opportunity we have. BHUVNESH SINGH: Okay. You are doing the same thing in your revenue guidance, if I look at the revenue numbers. Probably all the increase in guidance can be explained by the shift between rupee/dollar and rupee/pound or euro. So it does not even take into account extra volume growth that you have had in this quarter. Is there some reason behind that or -- I'm not able to figure out that,

why is that the case? B. RAMA RAJU: No, I think -- I think [inaudible]. V. SRINIVAS: No, this is whatever improvement in performance is not getting reflected is in on the guidance for the year at the bottom-line level. B. RAMA RAJU: Is that your question, Bhuvnesh? BHUVNESH SINGH: What I am trying to say is that if I take your full guidance and the new guidance on top line, on revenues, all the change can be explained just by the shift in currency, rupee/dollars and rupee/euro. So while this quarter we have had something like 4% extra volume growth compared to what we guided initially. So why haven't we changed our full year guidance by more than the impact of currency? V. SRINIVAS: No, no, no, no. That is not correct. There is definitely rupee impact. If you're talking about the bottom line, I think the question answer is to look at the U.S. GAAP guidance. There are items that reflect the current situation wherein we increased our [bottom] from 26 to 27.3. So that 1.3 is entirely because of the dollar growth versus rupee, what [inaudible] you're saying, out of that roughly 1.3% is because of the business or the up-tick we are seeing in Q1 that's already in the bag. And the balance is because of the rupee. So it is a combination. It is not that the entire increase is because of the rupee. BHUVNESH SINGH: So, on that, [have you built the] change in rupee/euro aspect, because the euro has appreciated much more strongly than dollars? V. SRINIVAS: Yes, it has appreciated more than 9% while dollars appreciated by around 3.4%. Well, what it was [inaudible] it's an average of all the currencies. BHUVNESH SINGH: Okay, so I'll maybe take it offline to understand that better. Thanks a lot. V. SRINIVAS: Yes. OPERATOR: At this moment, there are no further questions from participants here. I would like to hand over the proceedings back to Pratibha. OPERATOR: Thank you very much, April. At this moment, I would like to hand over the floor back to

the Satyam management for final remarks. V. SRINIVAS: Well, thank you, Pratibha. I would like to take this opportunity to thank every one of you for joining us and actually participating on the call. If you have any further questions, please do contact us. And if you want further information on the results for this quarter, they have been posted on our website, satyam.com. Please do visit us. You have an Investors link, and also all of the accounts are already included there. Please do look into it. And if you have any questions, please get in touch with our Investor Relations team. Thank you once again. OPERATOR: Ladies and gentlemen, thank you for choosing Webex Conferencing Service. That concludes this conference call. Thank you for your participation. You may now disconnect your lines. Thank you and goodnight.
